▶ Open the Book

"You may not become perfect, but at 10,000 hours, you’re guaranteed to be great."

PRESS PLAY · TUNE UP · TRUST THE CLOCK
Next →
Welcome

Learning Basic Electric Guitar

A nine-module path from picking up the guitar for the first time to jamming over backing tracks. Every diagram is lefty-mirrored with strings color-coded the same as Rocksmith - so what you see here matches what you'll see on screen when you play.

10K
HRS

Before You Pick Up the Guitar - The 10,000 Hour Rule

Look at the top-right corner of every page. ↗ that thing That's a countdown timer. It starts at 10,000 hours and ticks down every second you're actively practicing. Click it before you pick up the guitar. Click it again when you put the guitar down. The timer remembers your total across every session, every day, forever - it never resets unless you tell it to.

Why 10,000? The number was popularized by Malcolm Gladwell in his book Outliers, drawing on research by psychologist K. Anders Ericsson into what produces world-class expertise. The conclusion: roughly 10,000 hours of deliberate practice is the threshold that separates the great from the good. The same principle has been championed by leaders in disciplines from music to combat - including Gen. James "Mad Dog" Mattis, who built his entire philosophy of mastery on it: there are no shortcuts to expertise, only the discipline of showing up over and over. You may not become perfect. But at 10,000 hours, you're guaranteed to be great.

The math: 10,000 hours is roughly 1 hour a day for 27 years, 2 hours a day for 13.5 years, or 3 hours a day for 9 years. The point isn't to "finish" the timer - almost no one ever will. The point is to start the clock and trust that every minute you put in is moving the needle. The number doesn't lie. The clock doesn't lie. You and the timer keep an honest score.

Why it matters more than checkboxes: Checking a box says you "did the lesson." Banking an hour on the timer says you actually played the guitar. Two completely different things. One earns you the skill.

The one rule: Only run the timer when you're actually playing - not when you're watching tutorials, not when you're reading this page, not when you've put the guitar down to grab a drink. The number on the timer is your honest count. The only person who'd ever know if you cheated is you.

How to use this: Work through the modules in order. Each one has a short YouTube lesson, color-coded fretboard diagrams, and exercises. Tick the checkboxes as you go - your progress saves in this browser. Click the timer when you start practicing, click it again when you stop. Aim for 30–45 minutes a day, six days a week. You'll be jamming along to songs in two weeks. You'll hit 1,000 hours in about 5 years. You'll hit 10,000 in 27 years - at which point you'll be the kind of guitarist people drive across town to see play.

Hold the guitar, tune it, plug into the amp.

CAGED System

Five shapes that unlock the whole fretboard.

One finger bars the nut - play any chord anywhere.

Pentatonic / Lead

The one scale every rock solo is built from.

 7

12-Bar Blues

Shuffle in E - instant jam material.

Jam Tracks

1- and 2-chord backing tracks to play over.

 ⚡

Daily Routine

The 30-minute practice loop.

 📖

Chord Library

All chords on one page for quick reference.

How to read the diagrams

Every fretboard on this page is drawn the way you see your own guitar when you look down at it as a left-handed player:

  • Headstock is on the RIGHT
  • Frets count right-to-left (fret 1 is closest to the headstock)
  • Red string (low E, thickest) is on top
  • Purple string (high E, thinnest) is on the bottom
  • Numbers inside the dots are fingers: 1=index, 2=middle, 3=ring, 4=pinky
  • An ○ on the headstock side = play that string OPEN
  • A × on the headstock side = mute / don't play that string

↑ Example: this is an E major chord. Index on G string fret 1 (orange dot), middle on A string fret 2 (yellow), ring on D string fret 2 (blue), and the rest open.

🎸
Kurt Cobain
Nirvana - Lead Guitar & Vocals
⚡ Did You Know?

Cobain was left-handed but learned a few chords right-handed first - then flipped the guitar and restrung it. He never took a single formal lesson.

🎵
Jimi Hendrix
Guitar God - left-handed legend
⚡ Did You Know?

Hendrix played a right-handed Stratocaster strung upside-down for left hand - the exact opposite of how most people would even think to try. It changed the sound of rock forever.

← PreviousNext →

Which hand do you play with?

All fretboard diagrams update instantly.

Module 0 · ~25 minutes

Setup & Tuning

🎯
GoalHold the guitar comfortably, get it tuned to standard E A D G B E, and make a clean sound through your amp.

1. How to Hold a Left-Handed Guitar

Sit with the guitar on your right thigh, neck angled up about 15°, body resting against your stomach. Your right hand frets the strings (palm of the hand behind the neck, thumb on the back), your left hand picks/strums over the bridge pickup.

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 Play any guitar chord or scale - how to hold a guitar correctly · LEFT-HANDED lesson
Lefty tip: If you bought a right-handed guitar by mistake, you have three options: (1) buy a real lefty guitar - by far the best, (2) restring it as a lefty (works on most guitars, but the nut and saddle need filing), or (3) flip a righty over and play it strings-upside-down (Hendrix did this - but every diagram in the world will be wrong for you). This course assumes option 1 or 2: low E on top when you look down.

2. Parts of the Electric Guitar

Don't memorize the whole thing yet, just know these by name: headstock (with tuning pegs), nut (the white strip at the top of the neck), frets (the metal wires), fretboard (the wood you press on), pickups (magnets under the strings), volume / tone knobs, pickup selector switch, bridge, and output jack (where the cable goes).

3. Tuning Up

Standard tuning, from thickest to thinnest string: E A D G B E. Memory aid: "Eddie Ate Dynamite, Good Bye Eddie."

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 Marty Music - How to Tune Your Guitar For Beginners
Easiest tuning method: Free phone app - search "GuitarTuna" or "Fender Tune" in your app store. Pluck a string, the app tells you whether it's flat (turn peg to tighten) or sharp (turn peg to loosen). Re-check all six strings twice - tuning one string slightly changes the tension on the others.

4. Amp Basics for Day One

Three knobs you actually need: Volume, Gain/Drive, and Tone/EQ. Start with everything at noon (12 o'clock). Two channels: Clean (sparkly, undistorted - for chords, blues, country) and Drive/Lead (distorted - for rock, punk, leads). Plug in, channel on Clean, gain low, volume low, then bring volume up till you can hear yourself clearly without overpowering everything.

Your Strings at a Glance

This is the single most important thing to memorize before anything else. Look down at your guitar - the thickest string is closest to your chin (that's the 6th string, low E). The thinnest is closest to the floor (1st string, high e). In this book, we use Rocksmith's color system to make every diagram instantly clear:

These colors appear on every diagram throughout the entire book. Low E (red) is always at the top - that's how a lefty looks down at the guitar.

Memory trick: "Eddie Ate Dynamite, Good Bye Eddie." Say it out loud three times right now.

How to Hold a Pick

This sounds trivial. It is not. Get it right on day one:

  1. Curl your index finger loosely - like you're pointing, then relax it 30%.
  2. Place the pick on the side of your index finger at the first knuckle, angled slightly toward the tip. Not flat against the nail.
  3. Press your thumb pad on top. The pick should stick out about 5–8mm past the thumb. Barely visible.
  4. Other fingers: loosely curled underneath. Don't fan them out or grip with the middle finger.
  5. Tension check: Someone should be able to pull the pick out with moderate force. If you have to yank, you're gripping too tight. The pick should flex slightly on each strum.
Pick thickness: Start with a medium (0.73mm). Thin picks are floppy; thick picks are stiff. Medium is the learning zone. Later try 1.0mm for leads or 0.50mm for strumming sparkle.

The Chromatic Warmup - Do This Every Single Day

Run this exercise for 2 minutes before every practice session. It warms up all four fingers independently and builds the clean-pressing habit that every chord depends on.

Pattern: 4 fingers, 4 frets, repeat on every string

How: String 6, fret 1 (index) → pick. Fret 2 (middle) → pick. Fret 3 (ring) → pick. Fret 4 (pinky) → pick. Move to string 5, same 4-fret pattern. Continue through all 6 strings down, then reverse back up. Rule: never lift a finger until after you've picked the next one.

Start at 60 BPM. Press with fingertips right behind each fret wire. Keep un-used fingers hovering close to the strings. A slow clean run beats a fast buzzy one every time.

5. Amp Tone Guide

Your first week, keep it simple. These starting points work on most practice amps:

Sound GoalChannelGainBassMidTreble
Clean practiceClean3565
Warm bluesClean5–6654
Crunch rockDrive5–6566
Punk/metal chugDrive7–8747
Heavy metalDrive8–9738

Knob values on a 0–10 scale. Every amp is different - use these as starting points, then trust your ears.

✓Module 0 Checklist








🤘
Ricky 6ix (Sixx)
Mötley Crüe - Bass Guitar
⚡ Did You Know?

Nikki Sixx co-founded Mötley Crüe at 20 with literally zero bass experience. He bought a bass, learned three chords in a week, and started rehearsing. The band signed a record deal within two years.

🎸
Joe Perry
Aerosmith - Lead Guitar
⚡ Did You Know?

Perry is almost entirely self-taught. His main trick: slow down the record, match the note by ear, then speed back up. Every beginner should steal this method.

← PreviousNext →
Module 1 · 3–5 days to feel solid

Your First 3 Chords: E, A, D

🎯
GoalMemorize the shape of E, A, and D major. Be able to switch between them within ~3 seconds.

These three chords share a key (the key of A major) and let you play thousands of songs. They sound great on a clean amp setting with a touch of reverb.

<
▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 JustinGuitar - Beginner Electric Lesson 1 (orientation, then jumps into your first chord)

E Major

The biggest, fattest open chord. Strum all 6 strings.

Index (1) on the orange G string, fret 1. Middle (2) on the yellow A string, fret 2. Ring (3) on the blue D string, fret 2. Strum from low E down.

A Major

Three fingers crammed into one fret. The hardest of the three for most beginners - be patient, you'll get it.

All three fingers on fret 2: index on D (blue), middle on G (orange), ring on B (green). Don't play the low E string (the X over the red string). Some people use one finger to "barre" the three strings - try both ways.

D Major

The triangle. Once you get the shape it sticks for life.

Index on G (orange) fret 2, middle on the high E (purple) fret 2, ring on B (green) fret 3. Only strum from the D string down - don't hit the low E or A strings.

Playing them cleanly

Press just behind the fret wire - not on top of it, not in the middle of the gap. Press hard enough that the note rings; if you hear muting or buzzing, press harder or check that your fingertip isn't accidentally touching a neighbouring string. Curl your fingers so only the very tip touches the fretboard.

The Mechanics - How a Clean Chord Actually Works

A chord rings clean when four things are right at once. Beginners usually have one or two of them wrong, and that one wrong thing mutes or buzzes a string. Run this mental checklist on every chord:

1. Fingertips, not pads. Press with the very tip of each finger, hand curled like you're holding a small ball. If you press with the flat pad, the finger flops over and deadens the string next door.

2. Just behind the fret. The sweet spot is the space right before the metal fret wire (on the headstock side). Too far back and the note buzzes; on top of the wire and it chokes out.

3. Thumb behind the neck. Your thumb sits roughly in the middle of the back of the neck, opposite your fingers. It's the clamp that lets your fingertips press hard without your whole hand straining. A thumb hooked over the top robs your fingers of reach.

4. Press, then check, then strum. Place all the fingers, then pick each string one at a time to hear if it rings. Fix the dead one. Only then strum the whole chord. This habit - building the chord and auditing it note by note - is the single biggest accelerator for a beginner.

Fix the most common buzz: if a string buzzes, 90% of the time you're either (a) not pressing hard enough, (b) pressing too far from the fret, or (c) a neighbouring finger is lightly touching that string. Pick the dead string alone and wiggle each finger slightly to find the culprit. You'll learn more from fixing one buzz than from ten clean strums.

Troubleshooting - Why Strings Go Dead or Buzzy

Every beginner hits these exact problems. Here's the diagnosis for each:

"One string is muted/thuddy." A neighbouring finger is leaning on it. Curl that finger more and come straight down on its tip. Most common on the A chord, where three fingers crowd one fret.

"It buzzes even though I'm pressing." You're pressing in the middle of the fret gap. Slide the finger forward toward the fret wire (without going on top of it).

"My hand cramps after 30 seconds." You're squeezing way too hard, or your thumb is hooked over the top. Relax - you need far less pressure than you think. Press only until the buzz stops, no more.

"I can't get my fingers into A major." Totally normal - three fingers in one fret is genuinely cramped. Try angling your hand so the fingers stack diagonally, or use the one-finger mini-barre. It clicks within a week.

What to Listen For

A chord isn't "done" because your fingers are in the right spots - it's done when it sounds right. Train your ear for these:

Every intended string rings. Strum slowly and listen for any string that goes "thunk" instead of ringing. One dead string makes the whole chord sound wrong even if five are perfect.

No buzzing. A clean note is a pure tone; a buzz is a fuzzy rattle. If you hear rattle, it's pressure or finger placement.

The right strings only. On A you skip the low E; on D you skip low E and A. Listen that the muted strings stay quiet - a ringing wrong bass note muddies the chord.

It rings as one sound. A great chord sounds like a single rich event, not six separate notes. When all strings ring together cleanly, you'll hear it "bloom."

🎵 Theory Corner: why E, A, and D belong together. These three aren't a random starter set - they're the I, IV, and V chords of the key of A major (A=I, D=IV, E=V). Those three chords are the backbone of thousands of songs because they share notes from the same scale, so your ear accepts moving between them as natural. You'll meet I–IV–V in depth later, but know now: learning these three didn't just teach you three shapes, it handed you the skeleton of an entire key. The A–A–D–E loop below is literally a I–IV–V progression.
Anchor-finger head start: between A and D, your index finger barely moves - it stays near the same area. And both chords use a tight three-finger cluster. Treat the index as a light pivot and the change gets dramatically easier. (You'll use this same "anchor finger" idea everywhere in Module 3's chord-change work.)
▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 JustinGuitar BC-113 - E Chord (deep dive)

The "1-Minute Changes" Drill

This is the single most useful exercise in the whole course. It's how you stop "thinking" about chords and start "playing" them.

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 JustinGuitar - One Minute Changes (Stage 1)
11-Minute Changes - E ↔ A
  1. Set a timer for 60 seconds.
  2. Switch from E to A to E to A as fast and as cleanly as you can. Strum once each time.
  3. Count how many full changes you got in 60 seconds.
  4. Write the number down. Beat it tomorrow.

Beginners typically score 8–15 the first day, 25+ within a week, 40+ within a month.

21-Minute Changes - A ↔ D

Same drill. The trick: notice that your middle finger barely moves between A and D - keep it light on the strings as a pivot.

31-Minute Changes - D ↔ E

Same drill. Total: three different 1-minute drills per practice.

4Play a real song

The chords for thousands of songs. Try this 4-bar loop, 4 down-strums per chord:

A · A · D · E · (repeat)

This is the I–IV–V in A major - the bones of half of every classic-rock and blues tune ever written.

5The Place-and-Check Drill

For each chord (E, then A, then D): place all fingers at once, then pick each intended string one at a time, low to high. Every string must ring clean. If one's dead, fix it before strumming. Do this 5 times per chord. This is how you build the "audit your own chord" reflex that makes everything faster.

6The Air-Change Reps (no strumming)

Pick a pair (say E → A). Form E. Lift off, and form the whole A shape in the air, then land all fingers at once like a stamp. Lift, reform E. Do 20 of these per pair, slowly, focusing on landing all fingers together rather than one at a time. Seeing and planting the whole shape is the skill that separates fast changers from slow ones.

7The Perfect-Chord Ritual

Once a day, slow everything down and make one perfect E, one perfect A, one perfect D - every string ringing, no buzz, no dead notes, held for a full 4 seconds each. Quality over speed. One perfect rep teaches your hands more than fifty sloppy ones.

Practice Audio - Troy Stetina

🎵 TRACK Tuning Notes: E-A-D-G-B-E
0:00
✓Module 1 Checklist









🎸
Angus Young
AC/DC - Lead Guitar
⚡ Did You Know?

Angus plays a Gibson SG through a cranked Marshall - and that's basically it. No pedals, no effects rack. His entire iconic tone comes from turning the amp all the way up.

🎤
Justin Bonitz
Tallah - Vocalist & Co-founder
⚡ Did You Know?

Justin Bonitz (Season 2, Musician Mansion) fronts Tallah, the band he co-founded as a teenager. Tallah blends nu-metal intensity with jazz-influenced odd time signatures - their song 'No One Should Read This' hit 10M+ streams before they were 21.

← PreviousNext →
Module 2 · 5–7 days

5 More Chords: G, C, Em, Am, Dm

🎯
GoalAdd five more chords to your toolkit. After this module, you can play almost any pop, rock, country, or folk song ever written.
Suggested order: Em → Am → Dm → C → G. Em and Am are the easiest chords on the entire guitar - only two fingers each. Learn them first to build confidence.

Chord Diagrams

Study each shape below. The colored dots are your fingers - tap any diagram to hear it strummed.

* Em and Am use the same two-finger shape - just moved one string set over. That's your first "shape transfer" on guitar.

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 JustinGuitar - The 8 Essential Beginner Chord Grips

E minor (Em)

Two fingers, all 6 strings strummed. Beautiful, dark, easy.

Middle (2) on A string fret 2, ring (3) on D string fret 2. Strum everything.

A minor (Am)

Same shape as E major, just shifted up one string set. Don't play the low E.

Index (1) on B string fret 1, middle (2) on D string fret 2, ring (3) on G string fret 2. Strum from A down.

D minor (Dm)

Same triangle as D major, but the high note moves down one fret. Sad cousin of D.

Index (1) on high E fret 1, middle (2) on G fret 2, ring (3) on B fret 3. Strum from D down. Don't play E or A.

C Major

Pretty open chord - fingers staircase down across three strings.

Index (1) on B fret 1, middle (2) on D fret 2, ring (3) on A fret 3. Strum from A down (skip low E).

G Major

The biggest stretch you've done so far. Two common shapes - start with the "easy" 3-finger version.

Middle (2) on low E fret 3, index (1) on A fret 2, ring (3) on high E fret 3. Strum all 6 strings.

The "Big 8" Now Live in Your Hands

You now know the 8 chords every guitar player needs: E A D G C Em Am Dm. Memorize them cold. With these 8 shapes you can play most popular songs ever written. The next step is making the transitions between them invisible.

Finger-Pivot Mechanics - How the Pros Don't Move Their Hands

Great chord changes aren't about moving fast. They're about moving as little as possible. Almost every pair of common chords shares either a finger position, a finger shape, or a pivot note you can keep planted while the other fingers reposition around it. Learn these and your changes get smooth in days, not months.

The general rule: Before changing chords, ask yourself "what fingers do I get to keep?" The answer is almost always at least one. That finger becomes your anchor. Plant it first, then let the others swing into position. With practice, the anchor never leaves the strings at all.

1. Em → Am - the gift change

Easiest change on the guitar, because two fingers don't move at all. In Em your middle (2) and ring (3) sit on A-string fret 2 and D-string fret 2. In Am, they stay in the exact same place - you just add your index (1) on B-string fret 1. Don't lift, don't reset - just drop the index in.

2. C → G - the campfire change

This pair is in half of all pop songs ever recorded. The trick: your ring finger pivots. In C, it's on A-string fret 3. In G, it slides one string up to low-E fret 3 - same fret, same finger shape, just one string thicker. Don't lift the ring finger off the fretboard - keep it touching, slide it up. The other fingers reset around the moving anchor.

3. G → D - the country/folk staple

Bigger move (different shapes entirely), but there's still a trick: in G your ring finger is on high E fret 3. In D your ring finger is on B-string fret 3. Same fret, neighboring string. Make the ring finger your anchor - slide it down one string, then position the others. You'll instantly recognize the "Sweet Home Alabama" sound when you nail it.

4. C → Am - the verse change

Almost identical shapes, just one finger moves. In C: index on B-1, middle on D-2, ring on A-3. In Am: index on B-1, middle on D-2, ring on G-2. Index and middle don't move at all. Just slide your ring finger from A-string fret 3 to G-string fret 2. One finger. That's the whole change.

5. Am → Dm - minor cousins

Shift down one fret, keep the shape. In Am: index on B-1, middle on D-2, ring on G-2. In Dm: index on high-E-1, middle on G-2, ring on B-3. Your middle finger barely moves (G-string, just different fret). Train your hand to feel "Am" and "Dm" as variations of the same shape, not two unrelated chords.

6. Em → G - the rock anthem

Open up Em to G by adding two fingers, not replacing any. Em uses middle + ring on the A and D strings. G needs the middle to grab low-E fret 3, ring to grab high-E fret 3, and the index hops to A-string fret 2. The middle finger essentially slides from A-2 (Em) to low-E-3 (G) - close move. The trick is training your pinky to NOT join in (G is a 3-finger chord here, not 4).

7. D → A - the tiny pivot

You already know this one from Module 1, but it's worth recapping here as part of the system: in D your middle finger sits on high-E fret 2. In A your middle finger sits on G-string fret 2. Same fret, two strings apart. Use the middle finger as the silent pivot anchor.

The Drills

Do three different 1-minute change drills per practice session (not all of them - pick 3). Time yourself with a stopwatch. Count completed full changes (back-and-forth = 2). Write the score down. Beat it tomorrow.

11-Minute Changes - Em ↔ Am

Plant middle + ring. Drop index in for Am, lift it out for Em. Aim: 40+ changes in 60 seconds within a week. It's that easy.

21-Minute Changes - C ↔ G

Ring-finger pivot. Slide it between A-3 and low-E-3. Don't lift. Aim: 20+ in a week, 30+ in a month. This change is the hardest of the bunch but the most rewarded - half of all pop music opens up to you.

31-Minute Changes - G ↔ D

Ring-finger anchor on fret 3. Slides one string. Aim: 25+ in a week.

41-Minute Changes - C ↔ Am

Two fingers stay, one moves. This should be FAST after a few sessions. Aim: 35+ in a week.

51-Minute Changes - Am ↔ Dm

Same shape, shifted. Pay attention to the middle finger barely moving. Aim: 30+ in a week.

61-Minute Changes - Em ↔ G

The most important rock change. Aim: 25+ in a week. Once you have it, you can play full songs in minutes.

7Three-Chord Loop - Em → C → G (repeat)

Strum each chord 4 down-strums, then move. 60 seconds. Then bump to 80 BPM with a metronome. Mistakes are fine - the metric is "did the rhythm stay locked", not "did every note ring perfectly."

8Four-Chord Loop - G → D → Em → C

Same drill, four chords now. This is the "Don't Stop Believin'" / "Let It Be" / "Take Me Home, Country Roads" / "With or Without You" pattern - you'll see it again in the music section below. 4 strums per chord, 2 full loops, 60 seconds.

Putting It to Music - Your First Real Progressions

A chord progression is just a recipe for which chords come in what order. Almost all popular music sits on top of a small number of recipes. Here are the five you need first. Every one uses chords you already know.

Note on the Roman numerals: musicians label the chords in a key with Roman numerals so the same recipe can move to any key. I = chord built on the 1st note of the scale (the "home" chord). IV = on the 4th note. V = on the 5th. Lower-case = minor (so vi = minor chord on the 6th). Memorize the recipe in numerals once; you can play it in any key for life.

🎵 Recipe 1 - The Pop Progression: I – V – vi – IV

The most-used progression in modern pop music. The Axis of Awesome made a famous medley showing dozens of hits all built on this same four chords. In the key of G the chords are:

G · D · Em · C · (repeat forever)

Songs that use this exact pattern: Let It Be (Beatles), No Woman No Cry (Bob Marley), Don't Stop Believin' (Journey, with a verse offset), With or Without You (U2), Someone Like You (Adele), I'm Yours (Jason Mraz). Hundreds more.

How to practice: 4 down-strums per chord, then move. Set metronome to 80 BPM. Sing any one of those songs over the top - you'll hear it lock in.

🎵 Recipe 2 - The Rock Anthem: vi – IV – I – V

Same chords as the pop progression, different starting point. Feels bigger and more dramatic because it opens on a minor chord. In the key of G:

Em · C · G · D · (repeat)

Songs: Zombie (The Cranberries), Save Tonight (Eagle-Eye Cherry), Numb (Linkin Park, key shifted), countless punk and pop-punk songs.

🎵 Recipe 3 - The Classic I-IV-V (the 3-chord trick)

The bones of rock and roll, country, blues. Three chords forever. In the key of A:

A · D · E · (and back)

You already played this in Module 1. Now you can also do it in:

  • Key of G: G · C · D - try Sweet Home Alabama (G-D-C feel), Wild Thing if you shift, almost every country song
  • Key of D: D · G · A - try Three Little Birds (Marley), Bad Moon Rising (CCR)
  • Key of E: E · A · B - the blues lives here (B is a barre chord, but B7 works for now)

🎵 Recipe 4 - The Sad-Folk Classic: vi – IV – V – vi

Melancholy, hypnotic, opens on minor. In the key of C:

Am · F* · G · Am · (repeat)

*F is a barre chord - for now substitute Fmaj7 (no barre, just fret 1 on B + fret 2 on D + fret 3 on A + leave high E and G open) OR substitute Dm which sounds almost identical in this context.

Songs: House of the Rising Sun feel, Hotel California intro (key shifted), countless minor-key folk and indie.

🎵 Recipe 5 - The 12-Bar Blues

You'll cover this in depth in Module 7, but now that you know all 8 chords, here's the preview. In the key of E, the 12 bars (each bar = 4 beats):

E · E · E · E · A · A · E · E · B7 · A · E · B7

Just three chords (E, A, B7) but they create a complete musical statement. Tap any of the chord diagrams in this module's library to hear what each one should sound like, then string them together.

Why the Same Recipes Keep Appearing

It's not a coincidence. These progressions feel "right" because the chords share common tones - when you move from G to D, several notes from G live inside D, so your ear hears continuity instead of disruption. Across centuries of music, listeners' ears converged on a small set of patterns that feel resolved, tense, dramatic, melancholy, etc. The 8 chords you just learned unlock the bulk of that catalog.

You don't need to know why these recipes work to use them. But knowing they exist - that 5 progressions explain thousands of songs - is the moment guitar stops feeling like memorizing random shapes and starts feeling like speaking a language.

9The "I Know A Song" Drill

Pick any song you love. Look up its chords on Ultimate Guitar or any tab site. Match the chord names to the 8 you know. If they're all there (or mostly), play the song using 4 down-strums per chord. If a chord like F or B or D# shows up, skip it for now (or substitute Fmaj7 or B7 or use a power chord). The point of this drill is the realization that you can already play this song.

The Chord Shape DNA - See How They're Related

The 8 chords you know aren't random shapes. They come in major/minor pairs - each minor chord is its major twin with one note lowered by one fret. See it visually once and you'll never forget it.

Major vs. Minor - The One-Fret Difference

Major What changes Minor Feel
E Remove index finger (frees up G string) Em Brooding, open
A Add index on B string fret 1 Am Melancholy, classic
D Index drops from high-E fret 2 to fret 1 Dm Sad, tense

G and C don't have common minor versions in open position - their minor shapes require a barre. That's why we stop at Em, Am, Dm for now.

The Shape-Transfer Insight - Em and Am Are Twins

This is the single most important visual relationship for a beginner. Em and Am are the exact same two-finger shape, just moved one string set toward the floor.

Em vs Am - Same Shape, Different Strings

The two-finger core (middle + ring) sits in the exact same relative position both times - just shifted one string pair toward the treble. Am just adds the index finger on B string fret 1. That's the entire difference. When you "feel" Em, you already know Am's skeleton.

The Pivot-Change Visual Map

Every common chord change has an anchor finger - one finger that barely moves (or doesn't move at all) while the others swing around it. Here's the map at a glance. Find the change you're practicing and look at what stays still.

Chord Change Pivot Guide

Change Anchor (what stays) Movement Difficulty
Em ↔ Am Fingers 2+3 don't move at all Just add/remove index on B-1 ★☆☆☆☆ Easiest
C ↔ Am Index (B-1) and middle (D-2) stay Ring slides A-3 to G-2 ★★☆☆☆ Very easy
Am ↔ Dm Middle barely moves (same column) Shape shifts down one string set + fret ★★☆☆☆ Easy once felt
G ↔ D Ring slides high-E-3 to B-3 Other fingers rebuild around ring ★★★☆☆ Medium
C ↔ G Ring slides A-3 to low-E-3 Index and middle fully reset ★★★★☆ Hardest
Em ↔ G Middle shifts A-2 to low-E-3 Ring jumps to high-E-3, index to A-2 ★★★☆☆ Medium

Green anchor = finger that DOESN'T MOVE. Yellow anchor = finger that slides (same fret, different string) or barely shifts. This is what separates players who sound smooth from players who sound choppy - smooth players identify the anchor first, every time.

Practice Audio - Troy Stetina

🎵 TRACK Open Major Chords
0:00
✓Module 2 Checklist








🎸
Tom Morello
Rage Against the Machine - Guitar
⚡ Did You Know?

Morello graduated from Harvard with a degree in political science before becoming one of rock's most innovative guitarists. He uses his guitar's kill switch to create machine-gun rhythms that sound like turntable scratches.

🎸
Nik Nocturnal
Content Creator & Guitarist
⚡ Did You Know?

Nik Nocturnal (Season 2, Musician Mansion) went from bedroom YouTube covers to collabing with major metal acts. He's proof that consistent online content can build a genuine music career from scratch.

← PreviousNext →
Module 3 · 2 weeks to internalize

Strumming & Rhythm

🎯
GoalKeep a rock-solid beat with down and up strums - and switch chords without ever stopping your strumming hand. This is the skill that turns "knowing chords" into "sounding like a guitarist."

Here is the truth almost nobody tells beginners: rhythm matters more than chords. A player who only knows three chords but locks them to a steady beat sounds like music. A player who knows fifty chords but stutters and stops every time they switch sounds like someone practicing. This whole module is about getting onto the right side of that line. We will build the strumming engine first, then spend most of our time on the one thing that breaks every beginner: changing chords on time.

Why this is a 2-week module, not a 1-week one. Strum patterns you can learn in an afternoon. Chord-change timing takes real reps to wire into your hands - it is a physical skill, like learning to drive a stick shift. Be patient with it. The 10,000-hour timer in the corner is not a joke; this is exactly the kind of thing that quietly accumulates.

The Mechanics: Your Strumming Hand Is a Pendulum

Everything in this module rests on one idea. Your picking hand should move in a constant down-up motion - down on every beat, up on every "and" between beats - and it never stops, even on the strums where you don't actually touch the strings. Picture a clock pendulum, or a foot tapping. The motion is always there; strumming is just which swings happen to catch the strings.

Most beginners do the opposite: they move the hand only when they want a strum, so the hand starts and stops, and the timing falls apart. Get the pendulum constant first and every pattern below becomes "which strings do I miss?" instead of "what complicated motion do I make?"

The pendulum is the secret. Once your hand moves in constant down-up motion, every strum pattern is just "which down-ups do I miss the strings on?" Stop the motion and you'll lose time every single time. Keep the arm loose - the motion comes from the wrist and forearm, not a stiff elbow.

Step 1: Just Down Strums

Pick any chord. Strum down once per beat: 1, 2, 3, 4. Tap your foot on every beat. Do this for 60 seconds straight. The whole skill at this stage is staying steady - don't speed up or slow down. Boring? Good. Boring and steady is the goal.

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 JustinGuitar - Your FIRST Strumming Pattern EVER

Step 2: Down-Up "Eighth Notes"

Strum twice per beat - down on the number, up on the "and": 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 &. Your strumming hand becomes the pendulum from above: it never stops moving, even when you don't actually hit the strings. Say the count out loud while you play. Counting out loud is not optional at this stage - it is how the timing gets into your body.

Step 3: The Most-Used Strum of All Time

This single pattern is in thousands of songs across every genre:

D · D U · U D U

Counted: 1 (2) & (3) & 4 &. Your hand still pendulums on every 8th note - you just miss the strings on beats 2 and 3 (and the "and" of 1). If you can play this one pattern cleanly and switch chords under it, you can play a staggering number of songs.

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 JustinGuitar - The Most Common Strumming Pattern of All Time

Step 4: A Few Patterns Worth Owning

You don't need dozens. Four patterns cover almost everything. Notice they're all just the pendulum with different strings missed:

Folk / all-downs: D D D D - one down per beat. Simple, driving, never wrong.

The Most-Used: D · D U · U D U - the one from Step 3.

Pop ballad: D · · · D U · · - sparse, lets chords ring. Great for slow songs.

Driving rock: D D U · U D U - busier, pushes energy forward.

Strum Pattern Reference

Each row = one bar of 4/4 (8 eighth notes). D = down strum, U = up strum, dark box = hand moves but misses strings (ghost stroke - keep the pendulum going).

1. Folk / All-Downs - one per beat, never wrong

D
·
D
·
D
·
D
·
1 & 2 & 3 & 4 &

2. The Most-Used - D DU UDU - in thousands of songs

D
·
D
U
·
U
D
U
1 (2) & (3) & 4 &

3. Pop Ballad - sparse, lets chords breathe

D
·
·
·
D
U
·
·
1 · · · 3 & · ·

4. Driving Rock - DDU UDU - more energy, forward motion

D
D
U
·
U
D
U
·
1 2 & · & 4 & ·

Dark boxes are ghost strokes - your hand keeps its pendulum motion but the pick misses the strings. This is how the beat stays locked. If your hand stops on the dark boxes, timing collapses. Keep the pendulum going always.

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 JustinGuitar - Easy Strumming Patterns With Up Strums

🔄 The Heart of This Module: Chord-Change Timing

This is where everyone gets stuck, and where this module spends most of its energy. The problem is almost never the chords themselves - it's the gap. You strum a clean Em, you go to switch to G, and your strumming hand freezes while your fretting fingers scramble into place. That freeze is the sound of a beginner. The fix is not "switch faster." The fix is to never stop the strumming hand and to make the fretting hand move smarter.

The golden rule of changes: the strumming hand keeps penduluming no matter what. If your fingers aren't on the new chord yet, you strum muted strings or nothing - but the hand keeps its rhythm. A missed strum in time sounds musical. A frozen hand sounds broken. Protect the beat above all else.

Trick 1: The Air Change

Here is the single most important chord-change technique you will ever learn. On the last up-strum before the change, lift your fretting fingers off the strings early and move them through the air toward the next shape - landing them exactly as the new bar begins. You give up the last little bit of the old chord (nobody hears it) to buy yourself time to land the new one cleanly and on the beat.

In the Most-Used pattern (D · D U · U D U), the change happens on the downbeat of the next bar. So the final "U" of the bar is your launch point - fingers come up on that up-strum and fly to the new chord. Practice this in extreme slow motion at first. It will feel wrong for about three days and then suddenly feel obvious.

Trick 2: Anchor Fingers (don't lift what you don't have to)

Many chord pairs share a finger on the same string and fret. When they do, leave that finger down and pivot the others around it. Your hand has a pivot point, the change is smaller, and the shared note rings continuously - which actually sounds better, not just easier. The clearest example is Am → C: your index finger sits on the B string, 1st fret, in both chords. Don't move it. Ever.

Tap either diagram to hear it. Notice the index finger (1) on the B string, 1st fret is identical in both shapes - that's your anchor. When you change between them, that finger never leaves the string. The ring and middle fingers do all the moving.

Trick 3: See the Shape, Not the Fingers

Slow players think "index here, middle there, ring there" one finger at a time. Fast players see the whole shape as a single object and drop all the fingers at once, like a stamp. Train this by staring at the next chord shape in your mind before you move - then plant the entire hand in one motion. One landing, not three.

The "One-Minute Changes" Drill (the legendary one)

This is the most effective chord-change exercise ever devised, and it's brutally simple. Pick two chords. Set a timer for 60 seconds. Switch between them cleanly as many times as you can - no strumming, just place one chord, place the other, count each clean change. Write the number down. Tomorrow, beat it. Most beginners go from ~10 changes/minute to 30+ within two weeks. It works because it isolates the exact skill with zero distraction.

What to Listen For

Rhythm is judged by ear, not by eye. Train yourself to hear these four things - they are the difference between "technically correct" and "feels good":

1. The beat never flinches. Record 30 seconds on your phone and listen back. Does it speed up when it gets easy and drag when it gets hard? A steady tempo - even a slow one - beats a fast one that wobbles, every time.

2. Changes land on the beat. The new chord should arrive exactly on beat 1, not a hair late. A late change is the #1 tell of a beginner. If you hear a tiny "...uh" gap before each chord, that's the freeze - attack it with the air change.

3. The strum sounds even. Downs and ups should be roughly equal in volume and tone. Beginners usually hammer the downs and whisper the ups. Listen for a balanced, smooth wash.

4. It grooves - it makes you want to move. This is the real test. If your foot taps without you telling it to, you've got it. If it sounds like a metronome with strings, you need more dynamics (see the Feel Corner below).

🎵 Feel Corner: Dynamics make rhythm come alive. A robotic strum hits every strum at the same volume. A musical strum has shape: lean a little harder on beats 2 and 4 (the "backbeat" - it's where the snare drum lives in almost every pop/rock song), and lighten the in-between up-strums. You can also "ghost" strums - brush the strings with the pick while lightly muting with the fretting hand, so you hear the rhythm of the hand without a clear chord. Ghost strums keep the pendulum honest and add percussive groove. Loud-soft-loud-soft is the heartbeat of feel.

Exercises

1The Metronome Foundation (start at 70 BPM)
  1. Open a free metronome - Google "metronome", click the first result. Start at 70 BPM, not 90. Slower is harder and more honest.
  2. Play down strums on every click on E major for 60 seconds. Lock to the click - the click and your strum should sound like one sound.
  3. Add the up-strums between clicks (down-up-down-up). Keep the pendulum even.
  4. Layer the D–DU–UDU pattern on top, still glued to the click.
  5. Only bump the tempo up 5 BPM after a clean 60-second run. Earn every increase.
2One-Minute Changes (do this daily)

Set a 60-second timer. Pick a chord pair and switch cleanly as many times as you can - no strumming, just placing each shape. Count clean changes and log the number. Run it for each pair below, then beat yesterday's score tomorrow:

  1. Em ↔ G - the friendliest pair, great starting point.
  2. Am ↔ C - use the shared index-finger anchor; don't lift it.
  3. G ↔ C - the classic pair that's in a thousand songs and is genuinely tricky.
  4. D ↔ A - small fast moves, trains finger independence.
3The Air-Change Drill (slow motion)

Pick Em and G. Strum the D–DU–UDU pattern on Em for one bar. On the final up-strum, lift your fingers and float them toward G - land the G exactly on beat 1 of the next bar. One bar Em, one bar G, back and forth. Do it absurdly slowly at first (50 BPM). The win condition: the strumming hand never pauses and G arrives dead on the downbeat. Speed comes later - the motion comes first.

4The "Keep Strumming No Matter What" Drill

Put on any simple song you like at its real tempo. Strum all downs on the beat and try to change chords with it. Rule: your strumming hand is not allowed to stop, ever. If you don't have the next chord in time, strum muted/open strings in rhythm and catch the chord on the next beat. This rewires your brain to protect the beat instead of protecting the chord. It will sound messy for a week. Then it won't.

5The Backbeat Accent Drill

Play all-down strums on one chord, but hit beats 2 and 4 noticeably harder than 1 and 3. Count out loud: one TWO three FOUR. This is the single fastest way to make strumming stop sounding robotic. Once it feels natural on one chord, add it to a two-chord change.

Songs Built on These Changes

The best way to drill changes is inside real music. These four live entirely in chords you already know, and each one trains a specific change:

"Knockin' on Heaven's Door" - Dylan · G – D – Am, then G – D – C. Slow tempo, forgiving - the perfect first "real song." Trains G→D and D→C.

"Horse with No Name" - America · two chords, the whole song. The ultimate low-stress change practice with a real groove.

"Three Little Birds" - Marley · A – D – E. Bright, happy, and trains the D→A and A→E moves at an easy pace.

"I Saw the Light" - Hank Williams · G – C – D (more on this in the song sections coming soon). Boom-chick feel; trains G→C and the D resolution.

🎵 Theory Corner: Why these chords keep showing up together. Almost every song above is built from the I–IV–V chords of its key (you'll meet this idea in depth in the Music Theory chapter). In the key of G, that's G–C–D. In the key of A, it's A–D–E. Those three chords "belong" together because they share notes from the same scale, which is why your ear accepts the changes as natural. Learning to switch between the I, IV, and V of a key isn't just rhythm practice - it's learning the skeleton that thousands of songs are hung on.

Practice Audio - Troy Stetina

🎵 TRACK Strumming Pattern 1
0:00
✓Module 3 Checklist










Module 4 - Power Chords - the rock essential - is next.

🥁
Dave Grohl
Foo Fighters - Guitar & Nirvana Drums
⚡ Did You Know?

Grohl played drums on Nevermind with no click track - just pure feel. When Kurt died he locked himself in a studio and recorded every instrument on the first Foo Fighters album entirely alone.

🎸
Andrew Baena
Guitarist & YouTuber
⚡ Did You Know?

Andrew Baena (Season 2, Musician Mansion) built his fanbase making heavy guitar content on YouTube, known for his crisp tone and djent-influenced playing. He's one of the most subscribed independent metal guitarists online.

← PreviousNext →
Module 4 · The rock essential

Power Chords

🎯
GoalLearn the moveable two/three-finger shape that powers 90% of rock, punk, and metal - then play three real songs with it.

A power chord (the "5 chord", e.g. E5, A5, D5) is just the root note plus the 5th above it. No major, no minor - perfectly neutral, perfectly aggressive. Switch your amp to the drive/distortion channel and these come alive.

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 JustinGuitar - Your Ultimate Power Chords Guide for Beginners

The Shape (E5 - root on the low E string)

Open low E (red) plus index (1) holding A and D strings together at fret 2. Only strum those three strings. Mute the higher strings with the side of your index.

A5 - same shape, root on A string

D5 - same shape, root on D string

The Moveable Shape (e.g. G5 at fret 3)

Take the A5 shape and slide it up - your index finger barres the root, your ring finger plays the 5th two frets higher.

Index (1) on low E fret 3 (G note), ring (3) on A fret 5. This is G5. Slide the whole shape to fret 5 → A5. Fret 7 → B5. Fret 8 → C5. The root note name = the note your index finger lands on.

Root Notes on the Low E String

FretNotePower chord name
0 (open)EE5
1FF5
3GG5
5AA5
7BB5
8CC5
10DD5
12E (octave)E5

Palm Muting - The "Chug"

Rest the meaty edge of your strumming-hand palm just barely on the strings where they meet the bridge. The notes will become tight, percussive, and aggressive - that "chunk-chunk-chunk" you hear in punk and metal. Pull your palm away to let chords ring out fully.

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 Mastering Palm Muting on Guitar

Real Songs - Play These RIGHT NOW

Smoke on the Water - Deep Purple (the world's most famous riff)

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 Marty Music - How To Play Smoke On The Water

Smoke on the Water - Main Riff (Tab)

Played on the D string (blue). Each number = fret. Dashes = sustain. Played with a clean or slightly crunchy tone.

e |--------------------------------------------|
B |--------------------------------------------|
G |--------------------------------------------|
D |--0--3--5---0--3--6-5---0--3--5---3--0------|
A |--------------------------------------------|
E |--------------------------------------------|

Remember: as a lefty, your low E is at the top of the diagram when reading tab notation written for righty (the conventions are the same, just your guitar is mirrored). The D string (string 4, blue) carries the whole riff. Play it slow first - 0, 3, 5 pause; 0, 3, 6 bend-back to 5; 0, 3, 5 pause; 3, 0.

Seven Nation Army - White Stripes

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 Marty Music - Seven Nation Army Tutorial

Blitzkrieg Bop - The Ramones (3 power chords, that's the whole song)

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 Easy 3-Chord Punk - Blitzkrieg Bop tutorial · A5 → D5 → E5 → repeat. That's it.

Moveable Shape Mechanics - Why the Same 2 Fingers Play Every Power Chord

Once you've got the basic shape (index = root, ring or pinky = 5th two frets up), the same physical hand position plays every power chord there is. The shape never changes. Only the position shifts. Your index finger is the "name finger" - wherever it lands on the low E string tells you what the chord is called.

The Anchor Rule: When sliding between power chords, treat the shape as one unit. The hand moves as a whole - fingers don't separate, knuckles don't realign. It's a single chunk that hovers down the neck. Visualize it as one fat finger doing all the work.

Same Shape, Different String Set

There are two flavors of the moveable shape:

  • Low-E root - index on low E, ring/pinky on A string two frets up. Index name = the chord name. Examples: G5 at fret 3, A5 at fret 5, B5 at fret 7, C5 at fret 8.
  • A-string root - index on A string, ring/pinky on D string two frets up. Index name on A string = the chord name. Examples: C5 at A-fret 3, D5 at A-fret 5, E5 at A-fret 7, F5 at A-fret 8.

So most power chords have TWO playable positions. C5 lives at low-E fret 8 OR A-string fret 3. Same chord, different feel. The lower one (low-E root) tends to sound thicker and meatier; the upper one (A-string root) is brighter and easier to combine with other chords nearby.

Palm Muting Mechanics - Three Different Worlds

Palm muting isn't one technique. It's three - and the difference between them is the difference between Green Day, Metallica, and AC/DC. The physical change is tiny (where your palm sits on the strings). The sonic difference is huge.

🎸 The Punk Palm (Ramones, Green Day, The Clash)

Position: Palm rests so lightly on the strings it's almost not there. The strings still ring out, just with their attack slightly tamed.

Sound: Bright. Fast. Aggressive but open. Notes don't get "choked" - they get "punched."

Picking: Down-strokes only. As fast as your wrist can go. Johnny Ramone played entire shows at 200+ BPM doing nothing but down strokes on A5.

Example riffs: Blitzkrieg Bop, Holiday (Green Day), Should I Stay or Should I Go.

🎸 The Metal Palm (Metallica, Iron Maiden, Pantera)

Position: Palm pushes firmly against the strings right where they cross the bridge. Heavier pressure than punk.

Sound: Tight, dry, percussive. Each note becomes a "chug." When you lift the palm off, the chord explodes open by contrast.

Picking: Mostly down strokes for chugs, but the famous "gallop" rhythm (one open + two muted) needs alternation. The pattern: chug-chug-chug RING - chug-chug-chug RING. Listen to Master of Puppets intro.

Example riffs: Master of Puppets, Enter Sandman, The Trooper (Iron Maiden gallop), Walk (Pantera).

🎸 The Rock Palm (AC/DC, Foo Fighters, Black Sabbath)

Position: Medium pressure. In between punk and metal.

Sound: Dynamic. The whole point is the contrast between palm-muted verses (quiet, controlled) and full open chorus (loud, ringing). It's the dynamic shift that makes the song work.

Picking: Down-up alternation for verses, all-downs for chorus power.

Example riffs: Back in Black (verse muted, chorus open), Times Like These, Iron Man (slow, heavy, open).

1The Power Chord Slide
  1. Play G5 at fret 3 (low E root).
  2. Slide the same shape up to fret 5 → A5.
  3. Then fret 7 → B5.
  4. Then fret 8 → C5.
  5. Then back down: C5 → B5 → A5 → G5.
  6. Do it 4 times. Eight steady down strums per chord. Use distortion.
2The Anchor Finger Drill

Pick a power chord at fret 1 (F5). Slide it up without lifting your index finger off the string - fret 2, 3, 4, 5… all the way to fret 12. Then slide back down. The index finger drags. The shape never breaks. This is the muscle memory of every metal rhythm guitarist.

3The E5 ↔ A5 ↔ D5 Triangle

Switch between the three "open" power chords at 100 BPM, 4 strums each. E5 → A5 → D5 → A5 → E5. Notice these are the same three chords as the I-IV-V you played as full chords in Module 1, now in power form.

4Punk-Speed Down Strokes

Metronome to 160 BPM. Pick A5, hold it, strum nothing but down strokes on every click for 60 seconds. Then bump to 180. Then 200 if your wrist can take it. Punk wrist endurance is a real physical skill.

5Palm Mute Chug Pattern

E5, palm muted (metal palm pressure), 8 fast down strokes. Then 1 open ringing strum. Then back to muted chugs. Pattern: chug-chug-chug-chug-chug-chug-chug-chug RING. Loop 8 times.

6The Metal Gallop

One open down-stroke, two palm-muted down-strokes, repeat. DOWN · chug-chug · DOWN · chug-chug · DOWN · chug-chug. On E5 at 100 BPM. This is the Iron Maiden / Maiden-era heavy metal rhythm guitar mechanic.

7The Rock Dynamic - Verse to Chorus

Pick 2 power chords (say E5 and A5). Play 8 bars of palm-muted alternation (verse vibe - quiet, tight, controlled). Then 8 bars of open ringing alternation (chorus vibe - loud, full, open). The contrast is the whole song. AC/DC built a career on this.

Putting It to Music - Real Rock Songs Broken Down

You already know Smoke on the Water, Seven Nation Army, and Blitzkrieg Bop from the videos earlier in this module. Here are five more that use only chords you know. Each has a tempo, a palm-muting style, and a "moment" that makes the song.

🤘 Iron Man - Black Sabbath

Tempo: Slow (~70 BPM). Palm style: Rock - let it ring, dark and open. Power chords used: B5, D5, E5, G5 (all moveable shapes - slide between them).

The opening riff is essentially: slide B5 → D5 → E5 → D5, with the famous heavy bend at the start. It's slow, dark, and built almost entirely on letting power chords ring out. The moment: the iconic main riff is so well-known that just playing the first 5 seconds will turn heads.

🤘 You Really Got Me - The Kinks

Tempo: Medium-fast (~140 BPM). Palm style: Light punk palm. Power chords: F5 → G5 alternating (just two chords for the verse, then bumped up to G5 → A5 for the bridge).

Two-chord song that invented hard rock. Slide one finger up one fret. That's the entire song. Then slide both chords up two more frets for the bridge. Dave Davies famously sliced his speaker with a razor to get the distorted sound - but on your amp's drive channel, it'll sound great.

🤘 Bad Moon Rising - CCR

Tempo: Medium (~120 BPM). Palm style: Almost none - let the chords ring like a country song. Power chords (optional): D5, A5, G5 (you can also use full open chords D, A, G).

Classic rock pattern: D - A - G - D for verse. Up-tempo, all down strums, no palm muting. Great song to practice clean transitions because it's fast enough to challenge you but the chords are easy.

🤘 What I Like About You - The Romantics

Tempo: Fast (~150 BPM). Palm style: Punk-light - bright and bouncy. Power chords: E5, A5, D5.

Three-chord punk-pop perfection. The whole song is E5 - E5 - A5 - D5 (and a few variations). Pure I-IV-V in E. This is a "I learned guitar this week and can already play a real song" rite of passage.

🤘 Master of Puppets (intro) - Metallica

Tempo: Fast (~220 BPM). Palm style: Heavy metal palm. Power chord: E5 (mostly - the famous open-string thrashing).

The opening 16-bar palm-muted E pedal point is one of the most-played warm-up riffs in metal. It's literally just E5 chugged at high speed with the metal palm. If your wrist can do Exercise 4 above at 200 BPM, you can do this intro. Note: the rest of the song gets complex fast, but the intro is achievable now.

8Pick One Song - Play It Through

Choose any of the 5 songs above. Look up the rest of the chords/tabs on Ultimate Guitar. Play it through end-to-end, mistakes and all. The goal is the experience of "I played a whole real song," not perfection. Then play it again tomorrow. By the end of the week you'll know it.

9Make Your Own Riff

Pick any 3 power chords (e.g., E5, G5, A5). Arrange them in any order over 4 bars (16 beats). Use one of the palm muting styles. Loop it 8 times. Congratulations - you just wrote a riff. Every legendary rock song started exactly like this.

Practice Audio - Troy Stetina

🎵 TRACK Arpeggiated Chords 1
0:00
✓Module 4 Checklist










🤘
Tony Iommi
Black Sabbath - Guitar, inventor of metal
⚡ Did You Know?

Iommi lost the tips of two fretting fingers in a factory accident at 17. He almost quit music - but built plastic thimble tips, tuned down to reduce string tension, and accidentally invented heavy metal's downtuned sound.

🎸
Sophie Burrell
Bassist & Musician Mansion S2
⚡ Did You Know?

Sophie Burrell brought melodic bass playing and genuine rock energy to Musician Mansion Season 2 - proving that bass is a lead instrument when you play it like one.

← PreviousNext →
Module 5 · The unlock
🎵 TRACK Movable Power Chords: F5-Bb5
0:00

The CAGED System

🎯
GoalStop seeing the fretboard as random. Learn that EVERY major chord is one of five shapes - C, A, G, E, D - that you already know, slid into a new home. By the end you can find any major chord in five places on the neck.

You've already learned the five open shapes: C A G E D. Here's the secret nobody tells beginners: those five shapes are the entire fretboard. There is no sixth shape. Every major chord - anywhere, in any key - is one of those five shapes moved to a new fret and (usually) barred with your index finger. CAGED is the map that connects them.

Why CAGED is the holy grail: Once it clicks, three things happen at once. (1) You can find any chord in five places, so you're never stuck in one spot. (2) Barre chords stop being mysterious - they're just open shapes with your index finger playing the role of the nut. (3) Lead playing locks in, because every scale box you'll ever learn wraps around one of these five shapes. Rhythm and lead become the same map.

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 Paul Davids - The Truth About the CAGED System

The Mechanics - What's Actually Happening

An open chord has two kinds of notes: fretted notes (your fingers) and open strings (the nut is "fretting" them at fret 0). To move an open shape up the neck, you have to replace the nut with something - that something is your index finger laid flat across all six strings. That's a barre. The barre becomes a "movable nut," and the rest of your fingers keep the exact shape they had in open position, just shifted up by however many frets you moved.

So a CAGED shape is really two parts: the barre (the new nut) + the leftover shape (your other fingers). The fret your barre sits on, combined with which shape you're using, tells you exactly what chord you're playing - because the root note of the shape lands on a known string.

Theory Corner - Where the root lives. Every shape has its root (the note that names the chord) on a specific string:
• E shape → root on the 6th string (low E)
• A shape → root on the 5th string (A)
• D shape → root on the 4th string (D)
• G shape → root on the 6th string (and doubled on the 1st)
• C shape → root on the 5th string
If you memorize the low-E and A string note names (Module 1), you instantly know what every E-shape and A-shape barre chord is called. That's why those two are the workhorses of rock and punk.

The Five Shapes (all shown here as C major)

To prove they're all the same chord, every diagram below plays C major - just in five different places. Tap each one and listen: same chord, different voicing and texture.

The C Shape (open / lowest position)

Feel: bright, open, classic. This is the open C you already know - the "home base" of the system.

The A Shape (barred at fret 3)

Feel: punchy, mid-range. Root on the A string. This shape is the backbone of countless punk and rock progressions.

The G Shape (at fret 5)

Feel: big and full but a stretch - the hardest of the five to fret cleanly. Most players use it as a partial or a visual landmark more than a full grip.

The E Shape (barred at fret 8 - the standard barre chord)

Feel: thick, powerful, six strings ringing. Root on the low E. This is THE barre chord every rock player lives on.

The D Shape (at fret 10)

Feel: high, thin, chimey - only four strings. Great for adding sparkle on top of a band mix.

What to Listen For

This is the part most players skip - and it's the whole point. Tap the five shapes in order, low to high, and listen to how the same chord changes character as it climbs the neck. Low positions sound thick and dark; high positions sound thin and bright. Knowing this lets you choose a voicing for the mood you want, instead of always grabbing the same shape. A verse might want the dark E shape down low; a chorus might lift to the bright D shape up high. Same chord, totally different emotional weight.

Train your ear: play open C, then the A-shape C at fret 3, then the E-shape C at fret 8, one after another. Hum along. You'll hear the pitch center stay the same while the "color" rises. That's voicing - the single most underrated tool a rhythm guitarist has.

How They Connect - The Overlap

The shapes aren't islands. They interlock - the top notes of one shape are the bottom notes of the next. As you go up the neck the order is always C → A → G → E → D → (back to C an octave higher). That's the literal reason it's spelled "CAGED." Once you see one shape, the next one is always sitting right above it, sharing a fret or two.

C Major - All 5 CAGED Shapes on the Neck

Each colored zone shows one shape. Root notes (C) are highlighted with a white ring. The shapes interlock - the right edge of one shape is the left edge of the next.

All five shapes play C major. Circled notes = root (C). Color bands at bottom show each shape's fret zone. The order C→A→G→E→D always repeats up the neck - that's why it's spelled CAGED.

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 Paul Davids - How to actually USE the CAGED system

Variations - What Else CAGED Unlocks

CAGED isn't just for major chords. Once you know the five shapes you can flatten one note to get a minor version, lift a finger to get a 7th, or grab just two or three strings for a partial chord or a triad up the neck. The E shape and A shape minor barre chords (Em-shape, Am-shape) are the two most useful in rock and punk - they're the same grips as open Em and Am, barred and moved. And every scale box in Book Two wraps around one of these shapes, which is how rhythm and lead become one connected map.

Theory Corner - The two you'll actually use most. Honest truth: nobody plays full G-shape and C-shape barres in a fast song - they're awkward. The E shape (root on low E) and the A shape (root on A) cover 90% of real playing because their roots sit on the two easiest strings to track. Master those two cold; treat C, G, and D shapes as the connective tissue that lets you find notes and build slick voicings. That's how working guitarists actually use the system.

The E and A String Note Map - Your Fastest Tool

To use CAGED in real time, you need to know where the notes live on the two strings that matter most. Memorize this and you can find any major chord in the E shape or A shape instantly.

Low E String + A String - Notes at Every Fret

The note at each fret IS the chord name when you play an E-shape barre (on low E) or an A-shape barre (on A string). Sharps/flats shown both ways.

Bright red dot = natural note on low E. Plant your index there for an E-shape barre = that note is the chord name.

Bright yellow dot = natural note on A string. Plant your index there for an A-shape barre = that note is the chord name.

Quick drill: Say "I want G major." Find G on the low E string (fret 3) - play E-shape barre at fret 3. Then find G on the A string (fret 10) - play A-shape barre at fret 10. Two chords, same name, totally different feel. That's CAGED in action.

CAGED for Minor Chords - The Same Map, Dark Version

Good news: minor chords use the same CAGED shapes. You just use the Em shape instead of the E shape, and the Am shape instead of the A shape. The root locations don't change. The two you'll actually use are all you need.

The Two Minor Barre Shapes You'll Use 90% of the Time

Shape Root string Example What it looks like
Em shape barre Low E string Fret 3 = Gm, Fret 5 = Am, Fret 7 = Bm Open Em, barred. Index flat across all 6, middle+ring on the same 2 strings they always are in Em.
Am shape barre A string Fret 2 = Bm, Fret 5 = Dm, Fret 7 = Em Open Am barred. Index flat, middle+ring+pinky hold the minor three-finger cluster one string set lower.

The minor barre shapes feel tighter and more muscular than their open-chord counterparts - but if you can play open Em and Am, you already know the finger arrangement. The barre is the only new skill.

G Major in 5 Places - Full Walkthrough

You did C major in the exercises above. Here's the same journey for G major, so the system fully clicks. Every shape is G - same root, five personalities.

G Major - All 5 CAGED Shapes on the Neck

Notice the shapes are identical to the C major map - just shifted 7 frets lower (G is 7 semitones below C). The CAGED order never changes.

All five circles labeled G - same chord, five voices. The G shape starts open (that's the open G you already know). The most useful barre version is the E shape at fret 3 - that's the standard G barre chord most rock songs use.

The 2-Second Chord Finder. Any major chord, anywhere, instantly:
1. Do you want it on the low E string? Find the note name on the low E map above. Barre your index there. Play E-shape grip.
2. Do you want it on the A string? Find the note on the A string map. Barre your index there. Play A-shape grip.
3. Want it higher up the neck? Count frets up from either map. The note repeats at fret 12.
That's it. No more guessing. Every major chord you'll ever need lives in those two maps.

Songs That Live in CAGED

You're already playing CAGED whenever you use a barre chord. These show it off:

  • "Should I Stay or Should I Go" - The Clash: E-shape and A-shape barres sliding around. Pure CAGED punk.
  • "Bad Moon Rising" - CCR: open shapes, but try playing it with barre versions up the neck to hear the voicing change.
  • "Boulevard of Broken Dreams" - Green Day: built on movable shapes you now understand.
  • "Wish You Were Here" - Pink Floyd: watch how the same chords get re-voiced up the neck - that's CAGED thinking.

Exercises

1Find C Major in 5 Places
  1. Open C (C shape).
  2. A shape barred at fret 3.
  3. G shape at fret 5.
  4. E shape barred at fret 8.
  5. D shape at fret 10.

Don't worry about clean fretting yet - just find each one and watch the shape repeat. Say the shape name out loud as you grab it.

2Find G Major in 5 Places
  1. Open G shape (you know this - root fret 3, low E).
  2. E shape barred at fret 3 (the standard G barre chord).
  3. D shape at fret 5.
  4. C shape at fret 8.
  5. A shape at fret 10.

Notice it's the SAME five shapes as exercise 1 - just starting at a different fret because the root (G) lives in a different spot.

3The Two Workhorses

Pick any note on the low E string - say fret 5 (A). Play the E-shape barre there (A major). Now find the same A on the A string (fret 12) - too high. Instead find A on the A string at the open / fret 0 region and play the A-shape barre at fret 0... that's just open A. Practice flipping between the E-shape and A-shape version of the SAME chord so you always have two options for any chord.

Goal: for any major chord, you can instantly grab it two ways - E shape (root low E) or A shape (root A string).

4Voicing Swap (ear training)

Take a 2-chord song you know (say G and C). Play it once with open chords. Play it again with barre versions up the neck. Play it a third time mixing - open G, barre C at fret 8. Listen to how the feel changes with each voicing choice.

✓Module 5 Checklist









🎸
Eddie Van Halen
Van Halen - Guitar
⚡ Did You Know?

Van Halen built his own guitars from parts because he couldn't afford a decent one. His two-hand tapping technique was so new that other guitarists thought he was doing some kind of trick when they first saw it live.

🎸
Paula Carregosa
Guitarist - Musician Mansion S2
⚡ Did You Know?

Paula Carregosa brought classical guitar technique into a rock context on Musician Mansion Season 2 - her fingerpicking precision in a heavy music environment makes her stand out in any room.

The Five CAGED Shapes

Every shape below is a major chord you already know. Slide any of them up the neck (barre with your index finger on a new fret) and it becomes a different key. The root note is highlighted.

Pentatonic Box 1 Wraps the E Shape

The most important thing CAGED teaches you about lead guitar: Box 1 of the minor pentatonic always wraps around the E-shape barre chord. Find the chord shape - you just found your scale home base.

← PreviousNext →
Module 5+ · The neck unlocks

Barre Chords

▶ Watch on YouTube
🗝️
GoalUse one finger as a movable "nut" so you can play any major or minor chord anywhere on the neck - from just two shapes. This is the technique that turns the whole fretboard into chords.

You just learned CAGED - five movable shapes. Barre chords are the practical payoff of that idea: you take an open-chord shape, slide it up the neck, and use your index finger laid flat across the strings to replace the open strings (the job the nut was doing). That one finger becomes a movable nut. Suddenly the same shape gives you twelve different chords just by sliding it to different frets. Two shapes - the E shape and the A shape - cover every major and minor chord there is. This is genuinely a beginner wall, so we'll go slow and build it right.

Why barre chords are worth the struggle: open chords trap you down near the nut and only give you a handful of chords. Barre chords are movable - learn the F shape once and you can play F, F#, G, G#, A… all the way up, same shape. They're also how you play chords that have no easy open version (F, B, F#m, etc.). Push through the first two weeks of buzzing and your playing changes permanently.

The Mechanics - Your Index Finger Becomes the Nut

A barre chord has two jobs happening at once: the index finger bars across multiple strings at one fret, while the other fingers form a shape above it. Getting the bar clean is the whole battle. Here's how:

1. Roll the finger slightly onto its side. Don't press with the flat, soft pad of the index - roll it a touch toward the thumb side so you're pressing with the firmer edge. It hurts less and presses cleaner.

2. Keep the bar finger straight and close to the fret. Lay it right behind the fret wire, as straight as a ruler. The natural dips at your finger joints are where strings go dead - positioning near the fret minimizes the pressure you need.

3. Thumb dead-center behind the neck. The barre is a clamp: thumb pushing from behind, index pulling from the front. A thumb hooked over the top makes a clean barre nearly impossible. Lower the thumb.

4. Pull, don't just squeeze. A surprising amount of barre pressure comes from gently pulling the whole arm back (elbow toward your body), not from crushing with the hand. Let the bigger muscles help so your finger doesn't have to do it all.

1. The E-Shape Barre (the F chord and beyond)

Take the open E major shape, shift all the fingers up one set so the index is free, then bar across all six strings. At the 1st fret that gives you F major - the most famous "first barre chord" and the one that makes beginners cry. The root is on the low E string, so wherever your bar finger sits names the chord (bar at fret 3 = G, fret 5 = A, and so on).

F major (E-shape, barre at fret 1): index bars all six strings at fret 1; ring on A string fret 3, pinky on D string fret 3, middle on G string fret 2. All six strings ring. Root = the low E string, so this whole shape names itself off the bar fret - slide it up and it's F#, G, G#, A…

Start higher, not at fret 1. The 1st fret is the hardest place on the neck to barre (highest string tension, widest spacing). Practice the E-shape barre first at the 5th fret (that's an A chord) where it's much easier, then work your way down toward F as your hand strengthens. Everyone struggles with F at fret 1 - it's not just you.

2. The A-Shape Barre (the B chord and beyond)

Now take the open A major shape, free up the index, and bar across the top five strings. At the 2nd fret that's B major - a chord with no easy open version, which is exactly why this shape matters. Here the root is on the A string, so the bar fret on the A string names the chord (fret 2 = B, fret 3 = C, fret 5 = D…).

B major (A-shape, barre at fret 2): index bars strings 5–1 at fret 2; ring finger bars (or three fingers stack) D-G-B strings at fret 4. Don't play the low E. Root = the A string, so slide it up the neck for C, C#, D, and beyond.

🎵 Theory Corner: two shapes, every chord. The E-shape barre has its root on the low E string; the A-shape barre has its root on the A string. Because you memorized the note names on those two strings (or will), you can find any major chord two different ways. Want a G? E-shape barred at fret 3, OR A-shape barred at fret 10. Same chord, two places, different voicing. And the minor versions are tiny tweaks: lift one finger off the E-shape to get E-shape minor (that's your Fm, Gm, etc.), and adjust the A-shape similarly for Am-shape barres. Two major shapes plus their two minor variants = literally every basic chord on the guitar.

Making Minor Barre Chords

You don't learn new shapes for minors - you tweak the two you have. E-shape minor: play the E-shape barre but lift the finger that was on the G string (the major 3rd), letting the bar cover it instead. That one change turns F into Fm, G into Gm, anywhere up the neck. A-shape minor: similarly flatten the note on the B string by one fret. Same root strings, same naming logic - so learning major automatically gets you minor for free.

What to Listen For

Barre chords are pass/fail by ear - one dead string under the bar and the whole chord sounds wrong. Train these:

Every string rings, especially the thin ones. Strum slowly, string by string. The strings under the tip end of your bar (the high strings) die most often because your fingertip dips there. If the high E or B "thunks," roll the finger or press nearer the fret.

No muted middle strings. The shape fingers above the bar can accidentally lean on a string. Pick each one to check.

It rings as a full, even chord - not a thin two-or-three-note thing. A clean barre sounds as rich as an open chord; a struggling one sounds thin and choked.

Exercises

1The High-Fret Warmup

Play the E-shape barre at the 7th fret (a B chord) and just hold it, picking each string one at a time to check it rings. Then 6th fret, 5th, 4th… walking down toward F. The lower you go the harder it gets - stop where it gets too tough today and come back tomorrow. Your hand strengthens fast.

2The Bar-Only Drill

Forget the shape fingers. Just lay your index across all six strings at fret 5 and strum. Get that ringing cleanly first - roll the finger, adjust pressure, find the angle where all six speak. The bar is 90% of the difficulty; master it alone before adding the rest.

3One Shape, Up the Neck

Play the E-shape barre and move it: fret 1 (F), fret 3 (G), fret 5 (A), fret 7 (B). Same shape, four chords, just sliding. Feel how the movable shape gives you the whole neck from one memorized form.

4Barre a Real Progression

Play F · G · C · using all barre chords: F = E-shape fret 1, G = E-shape fret 3, C = A-shape fret 3. Switching between an E-shape and an A-shape barre is a real workout - go slow, keep the strum going (Module 3's "never stop the hand" rule applies here too).

Practice Audio - Troy Stetina

🎵 TRACK Open Power Chords 1
0:00
✓Barre Chords Checklist








Note Names on the Low E and A Strings

To name a barre chord, you need to know the note your bar finger is on. Here are all the natural notes (no sharps/flats) up to fret 12. The highlighted frets (3, 5, 7, 9, 12) have fret dots to help you navigate quickly.

Green = important landmarks (G, C, B, E). Orange = most useful barre positions (A, D at fret 5 / D, G at fret 10). Fret 12 = open strings, one octave higher. The neck repeats after fret 12.

With CAGED and barre chords together, you can now play any chord anywhere on the neck. Module 6 - Pentatonic & Lead - turns to soloing next.

🎩
Slash
Guns N' Roses - Lead Guitar
⚡ Did You Know?

Slash's iconic top hat was a last-second prop from a costume shop the night before a music video shoot. He kept wearing it for 30 years. His main guitar - a Les Paul - was found by chance in a pawn shop in LA.

🎸
Rudy Ayoub
Guitarist & Online Educator
⚡ Did You Know?

Rudy Ayoub (Musician Mansion S2) is known for combining shred technique with strong music theory - one of the few online guitarists who can explain WHY something sounds cool, not just play it fast.

← PreviousNext →
Module 6 · Lead guitar starts here

Pentatonic Scale & Lead Basics

🎯
GoalMemorize the A minor pentatonic scale, position 1. With this single shape you can solo over almost any blues, rock, or punk song in A or C.

The minor pentatonic scale is five notes per octave (penta = 5). It's the scale Jimmy Page, Slash, Hendrix, BB King, Eric Clapton, Jack White and probably your favorite guitarist all use 80% of the time. One position, infinite music.

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 The A Minor Pentatonic Scale (1st Position)

A Minor Pentatonic - Position 1 (Box 1)

Starts at fret 5 on the low E string. Two notes per string. The diagram below shows every note in the box.

Use index (1) for the lower note on each string and ring (3) for the upper. The dots circled brighter are the root notes (A) - these are your "home" notes.

YOUR FIRST LICK - "The Hello Blues"

Five notes. Sounds like a real lead guitarist. Here's the diagram - the numbers in the dots are the order you play them, not finger numbers. (Use index for fret 5, ring finger for fret 7.)

Sequence: 1 Pick G string fret 7 (orange) → 2 Bend it up a whole step (push the string up toward the ceiling until the pitch matches fret 9) → 3 Release the bend back down → 4 Pick G string fret 5 → 5 Pick D string fret 7 (this is the A root - your "home").

The bend is the secret. Use your ring finger to push the string, with your middle and index fingers backing it up behind it on the same string for strength. Push toward the ceiling, not toward the floor. If the string buzzes out, you bent too far. If it doesn't change pitch, push harder.

YOUR SECOND LICK - "The Walk Home"

A descending run that ends on the A root. Builds tension, then resolves. Play it slow at first.

Sequence: 1 G string fret 7 → 2 G string fret 5 → 3 D string fret 7 → 4 D string fret 5 → 5 A string fret 7 → 6 A string fret 5 (a low A, deep and dark - the home). Hold the last note for 2 full seconds.

YOUR THIRD LICK - "Blues Question & Answer"

Two phrases. The first asks a question (ends UP, unresolved). The second answers it (ends DOWN on the root). This is the entire structure of blues melody in two licks.

Question (notes 1–4): A string fret 5 → A string fret 7 → D string fret 5 → D string fret 7. Pause for 1 second. Answer (notes 5–8): G string fret 7 (bend & release) → G string fret 5 → D string fret 7 → A string fret 7 (root, hold).

How to Practice It

  1. Play the scale ascending - low note to high note, one note at a time.
  2. Play it descending - high note back down.
  3. Repeat both ways at 60 BPM, one note per click.
  4. Bump to 80 BPM. Then 100. Then 120.
  5. Now play it freely - pick any notes in the box, in any order. This is improvisation.

Bend, Slide, Hammer, Pull - The Four Lead Tools

Bend: Push a fretted string upward (or downward, away from your body) so the pitch raises. Slide: Pick a note and slide your finger to a higher fret without re-picking. Hammer-on: Pick a note then "hammer" a higher fret with another finger to sound it without re-picking. Pull-off: The reverse - pluck a fretted note, then pull your finger off so the lower note sounds. Mix these into your pentatonic noodling and it instantly sounds like real lead guitar.

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 Is ONE position of the Minor Pentatonic enough? - Marty Schwartz
1Memorize the Box
