  • They have been heard so many times in famous songs that listener brains recognize them as "guitar music." Familiarity is musical comfort.
  • They reward bending and vibrato - every one of these licks sounds better with technique applied. Pentatonic exercises do not.

The reason 50 years of blues and rock guitarists keep coming back to the same vocabulary is not lack of imagination - it is that this vocabulary works. The notes hit the right places. The rhythms feel good. The shapes fit the human hand.

✓Chapter 5 Checklist










Chapter 6 - Soloing Over Chord Changes - coming up. Now you have vocabulary; next is knowing when to use which word.

🎸
Guthrie Govan
Guitarist & Educator
⚡ Did You Know?

Guthrie Govan is widely considered one of the most technically complete guitarists alive. He can play in any style at any tempo - but he says the most important thing he ever learned was how to make one note count.

🥁
Alexa Rae
Drummer - @alexa.drums / Musician Mansion S2
⚡ Did You Know?

Alexa Rae plays with a ferocity that makes heavy bands instantly tighter. Her online clips consistently go viral because she makes technically brutal drumming look like she's having the time of her life - because she is.

▶ Quick Workout: Lick Rotation

Pick any 3 licks from this chapter - one call-and-response style, one fast run, one slow expressive one. Set a timer for 90 seconds per lick. Play each one on loop, super slow, then gradually speed up until you hit the wall. Note where the wall is (which beat falls apart). That's tomorrow's practice target.

The rotation matters: switching between lick types every 90 seconds keeps your brain engaged and prevents the "autopilot loop" where you drill something fast but never really hear it. Stay slow enough that every note rings clean.

← PreviousNext →
Book Two · Chapter 6
🎵 TRACK Barre Chord Progression 1
0:00

Soloing Over Chord Changes

▶ Watch on YouTube
🎯
GoalWhen the backing chord changes, your solo should follow it. Learn to target chord tones of whichever chord is playing - the trick that turns a "scale player" into a "musician who plays guitar."

So far you have been soloing in A minor pentatonic over backing tracks that stay in A. The scale works for the whole song because every chord in the backing is in the key of A. That is the easy mode. Every note you play is "in the key," so nothing clashes.

But real songs have CHORD CHANGES. The verse uses Am-Dm-Am-E7. The chorus uses C-F-G-C. A bridge might modulate to a completely different key. If you keep soloing the same way over all these different chords, some notes start to clash - they fight the chord underneath instead of completing it.

The pros adjust. They target different notes depending on which chord is sounding underneath them. This chapter teaches you how.

Two Approaches - Static vs Dynamic

🟨 The Static Approach - "One Scale for the Whole Song"

Pick one scale that fits the whole key (A minor pentatonic for an A blues). Use it the entire time regardless of which chord is playing.

When it works: When the song has few chord changes (e.g., a 1-chord vamp), or when all chords share many notes (most blues progressions). Most beginner-friendly. This is what you have been doing.

When it falls flat: Progressions with big tonal shifts (major/minor in the same song, key changes mid-solo, modal pieces). Your scale runs out of "safe" notes and starts hitting tension notes randomly. Sounds aimless.

The trap: You feel locked in the scale. Your solo never seems to "land" anywhere. Listeners feel the disconnect even if they cannot articulate it.

🟩 The Dynamic Approach - "Follow the Chords"

Adjust your note choices when the chord changes. Specifically, land on a CHORD TONE of whichever chord is currently sounding. Chord tones are the root, 3rd, and 5th of the chord - the notes that make the chord what it is. Landing on them creates the "I am following the song" feeling that separates pros from beginners.

The skill levels:

  • Level 1 (beginner): Target only the root of each chord. Land on it at the start or end of each phrase.
  • Level 2 (intermediate): Target any chord tone (root, 3rd, OR 5th). More landing options.
  • Level 3 (advanced): Target "guide tones" - the 3rd and 7th of each chord, which define the chord character even more than the root. Book Three material.

For this chapter, focus on Level 1. Just learn to land on the ROOT of whichever chord is playing. That alone will transform how your solos sound.

Why Chord-Tone Targeting Works

Your ear hears each chord as a sonic "frame." When you play a note that lives INSIDE the frame (a chord tone), it sounds consonant - your brain processes it as "fitting in," like a word that belongs in the sentence. When you play a note OUTSIDE the frame (in the scale but not in the current chord), it sounds dissonant - like a word that almost fits but creates friction.

Both inside and outside notes have a place in real music. Pros use outside notes for tension (forward motion, momentum) and inside notes for resolution (relief, landing). The rhythm is: tension → tension → tension → RESOLUTION. The resolution is almost always on a chord tone.

You do not have to land on a chord tone EVERY note - that would sound predictable. Just on the IMPORTANT notes - start of a phrase, end of a phrase, long held notes. The notes in between can still be from your normal scale. Think of chord tones as the "punctuation" in your musical sentence: periods, exclamation points. The other notes are just words.

Chord-Tone Maps for the Three Most Common Blues Keys

The Key of A (what you have been jamming on)

Standard 12-bar blues in A uses three chords: Am (I), Dm (IV), E7 (V).

ChordPlayed in barsChord tonesWhere they sit in Box 1
Am (I)1, 2, 3, 4, 7, 8A · C · EA: D-string fret 7 · C: G-string fret 5 · E: A-string fret 7
Dm (IV)5, 6, 11D · F · AD: A-string fret 5 · A: D-string fret 7
E7 (V)9, 12E · G♯ · B · DE: A-string fret 7 · D: A-string fret 5 · G♯: low E fret 4 (leading tone!)

The Key of G (very common for rock)

ChordChord tonesWhere they sit (G minor pentatonic Box 1 at fret 3)
Gm (I)G · B♭ · DG: low E fret 3 · B♭: A-string fret 1 · D: D-string open or A-string fret 5
Cm (IV)C · E♭ · GC: A-string fret 3 · G: low E fret 3 · E♭: D-string fret 1
D7 (V)D · F♯ · A · CD: D-string open · A: A-string open · F♯: low E fret 2

The Key of E (the deepest blues key)

ChordChord tonesWhere they sit (E minor pentatonic at fret 12 or open)
Em/E7 (I)E · G · BE: low E open or fret 12 · G: low E fret 3 or fret 15 · B: A-string fret 2 or fret 14
A7 (IV)A · C♯ · E · GA: low E fret 5 · E: low E open · C♯: A-string fret 4
B7 (V)B · D♯ · F♯ · AB: A-string fret 2 · D♯: A-string fret 6 · F♯: low E fret 2

Notice across all three tables: common tones show up in multiple chords. The A note is a chord tone for both Am AND Dm in the key of A. The C note is a chord tone for both Am AND F in the key of C. Common tones are your safety net - landing on them works no matter which chord just changed underneath you.

5 Worked Examples

🎯 Phrase 1 - Targeting Am

Three-note phrase that lands on A (the Am root). Use when the backing is on Am.

Sequence: G-string fret 7 (D) then G-string fret 5 (C) then D-string fret 7 (A). The phrase resolves DOWN onto the Am root. Hold the last note with vibrato.

🎯 Phrase 2 - Targeting Dm

Four-note phrase that lands on D (the Dm root). Use when the backing changes from Am to Dm.

Sequence: B-string fret 5 (E) then G-string fret 5 (C) then D-string fret 7 (A) then A-string fret 5 (D). Walks down to the deep D note.

🎯 Phrase 3 - Targeting E7

Three-note phrase that lands on E (the E7 root). Use when the backing hits the V chord.

Sequence: D-string fret 5 (G) then D-string fret 7 (A) then A-string fret 7 (E). Lands on E - the V chord root.

🎯 Phrase 4 - The V to I Resolution (most satisfying move in music)

Here is the dominant pull. The G♯ note (low E fret 4) is the "leading tone" - one fret below A. When E7 is the V chord and we are heading back to A as the I, G♯ wants to PULL upward to A like a magnet. This 3-note lick demonstrates it.

Sequence: A-string fret 7 (E - V root, played first to establish E7) then low E fret 4 (G♯ - the leading tone) then low E fret 5 (A - RESOLVED to I root). Play the last two notes connected, like the G♯ is "falling into" A. Massively satisfying.

🎯 Phrase 5 - The Common-Tone Cheat (A holds across Am AND Dm)

Sometimes the simplest move is the best. Hold the A note (D-string fret 7) across the chord change from Am to Dm. A is a chord tone for both. It feels resolved over BOTH chords. No new diagram needed - just hold D-string fret 7 with vibrato across 2 bars of Am then 2 bars of Dm. Sounds like a master.

The V to I Pull - Why It Is the Most Satisfying Move in Music

This deserves its own section. In a major or minor key, the V chord contains a note called the leading tone - the note one half step below the I chord root. In the key of A, the V chord is E7. The G♯ in E7 is the leading tone - one half step below A.

When the V chord resolves to the I chord, the leading tone (G♯) RISES to the tonic (A) by a half step. This single half-step move is hard-wired into the human ear as maximum resolution. It is the most satisfying interval movement in Western music. Every classical cadence ends with a V to I. Every blues turnaround uses it. Every pop song with a "lifted" feeling at the chorus is leveraging it.

As a soloist, when the backing moves from V (E7) to I (Am), if you play a phrase that LANDS on G♯ over the E7 and then SLIDES UP to A as the chord changes, you will get the strongest sense of resolution available to a guitarist. This is Phrase 4 above. Use it.

The Mixolydian Trick (Bonus)

Once you can target chord tones, here is a pro move: over the V chord (E7 in A), briefly switch from A minor pentatonic to A MAJOR pentatonic. The major pentatonic adds G♯ (instead of G) and C♯ (instead of C), which match the E7 chord better. The G♯ is literally the major 3rd of E7. Sounds bluesy and sophisticated - Albert King, BB King, John Mayer all do this constantly.

Quick demo: when E7 is sounding (bars 9 and 12 of an A blues), play any note from Box 1 BUT raise the G to G♯ (move D-string fret 5 up to fret 6, or G-string fret 7 up to fret 8). Just on those bars. Then switch back to regular A minor pentatonic when Am returns. You will instantly sound more "blues-aware." Book Three covers this in full.

Real Songs That Demonstrate Chord-Tone Targeting

  • BB King - "The Thrill Is Gone" - masterclass in landing on chord tones. Every phrase ends on the root of the current chord.
  • Eric Clapton - "Crossroads" (Cream live) - follows every chord change obsessively. Listen for how each phrase pivots when the chord moves.
  • Brian May - "Bohemian Rhapsody" guitar solo - every note targeted. Brian arranged the entire solo around the chord progression.
  • David Gilmour - "Comfortably Numb" second solo - slower tempo lets you HEAR the chord-tone targeting clearly.
  • Stevie Ray Vaughan - "Pride and Joy" - every turnaround lands on the V to I resolution.

The Practice Plan

1One Phrase Per Chord (the foundation drill)

12-bar blues backing track in A. Play ONE phrase per chord, each landing on that chord root:

  • Bars 1–4 (Am): Phrase 1 → land on A.
  • Bars 5–6 (Dm): Phrase 2 → land on D.
  • Bars 7–8 (Am): Phrase 1 again → A.
  • Bar 9 (E7): Phrase 3 → land on E.
  • Bar 10 (Dm): Phrase 2 → D.
  • Bars 11–12 (Am to E7): Phrase 1 then Phrase 3.

Slow at first. Look at the chord-tone table while you play. This single drill is what builds the "following the changes" instinct. Do it once per practice session for a week and you will start hearing the chord changes in your head before they happen.

2The Common-Tone Lazy Mode

Same backing track. Play ONLY the note A (D-string fret 7) over EVERY chord. Hold it. Add vibrato. Vary the rhythm. A is a chord tone for both Am AND Dm, so this works for 10 of the 12 bars. (For E7 in bars 9 and 12, slide A up to B on the same string - B is a chord tone of E7.) Boring but instructive - proves common tones are real.

3Mix Vocabulary + Targeting

Backing track on. Use your Chapter 5 licks BUT consciously land each lick on a chord tone of whatever chord is playing. Start a lick whenever the chord changes. End it on the chord root. This is what real solos sound like - vocabulary AND targeting working together.

4The 4-Beat Target

Stricter version of drill 1. Backing track on. Land on a chord tone exactly on beat 1 of every chord change. Doesn't matter what you play in between - but beat 1 of every chord MUST be a chord tone. Trains your sense of "where you are in the form."

5Resolve the V to I

Backing track on. EVERY time the V chord (E7) appears in bars 9 and 12, play Phrase 4 (the V to I resolution lick) so the G♯ to A move happens right as the chord changes. The dominant pull will hit listeners like a punch. This drill alone makes any blues solo sound 50% more "professional."

612-Bar in Another Key (G blues)

Find a G blues backing track on YouTube. Use the key-of-G chord-tone table above. Play "one phrase per chord" - land on G over Gm, C over Cm, D over D7. Transposing forces you to actually think about chord tones rather than relying on muscle memory in Box 1. Once you can do this in two keys, you can do it in any key.

7The Target Listening Drill

Pull up "The Thrill Is Gone" by BB King on YouTube. Listen to the first guitar solo with the chord chart open (search "Thrill Is Gone chords"). Try to hear when BB King lands on the chord root. You will start to hear it everywhere. Once you can spot chord-tone targeting in real solos, you will start doing it instinctively in your own.

Theory Corner - Why This Works on the Brain

From the theory chapter: every chord is built from a stack of three notes (root + 3rd + 5th). When those notes sound together, the listener ear hears them as a unified harmonic frame. It is not three separate notes - it is one chord shape.

When your solo plays a note that is INSIDE that frame, the brain processes it as "this note is already in the chord" - it is reinforcing what is already there. Consonance. Resolution. When you play a note OUTSIDE the frame, the brain processes it as "this note is fighting the chord" - there is friction, momentum, the music wants to MOVE. Dissonance. Tension.

The genius of chord-tone targeting: using both. Outside notes in the middle of phrases push the listener forward; inside notes at phrase ENDS provide the relief that makes the journey feel complete. Tension and release. This is the engine of all musical phrasing in every style of music ever.

The V to I move (Phrase 4) is the strongest version of this engine: the leading tone (G♯) is OUTSIDE the I chord (Am), so it generates tension; then it rises one half-step to A, the root of I, so it fully resolves. Outside note to inside note in one half-step move = max payoff for min motion. No wonder it has been the dominant cadence in Western music for 500 years.

✓Chapter 6 Checklist









Chapter 7 - Improvisation Sessions - coming next. Now you have vocabulary AND you can follow the changes; the next step is long-form jamming that ties it all together.

🎸
Mark Knopfler
Dire Straits - Guitar
⚡ Did You Know?

Knopfler plays with his fingers - no pick. He developed his signature tone by accident: he couldn't afford a proper pick as a teenager, so he used his thumb and fingers on the strings. That 'mistake' became one of the most identifiable guitar sounds in history.

🎸
Vince Mindas
Musician Mansion S2
⚡ Did You Know?

Vince Mindas (Musician Mansion S2) is the kind of guitarist who finds the note nobody in the room expected - drawing from jazz, fusion, and rock to build solos that take turns most players wouldn't dare make.

▶ Quick Workout: Chase the Root

Play a simple 2-chord progression: Am to G, one bar each. When you're on Am, stay near the A root note (string 5 fret 0 or string 3 fret 2). When you shift to G, land on a G note (string 6 fret 3, string 4 open, or string 1 fret 3).

You don't have to play the root on beat 1 every time - just make sure it appears somewhere in each bar. This one habit instantly makes solos sound intentional over chord changes instead of random. Root awareness is the bridge between scale running and actual melody.

← PreviousNext →
Book Two · Chapter 7
🎵 TRACK Fingerpicking Style
0:00

Improvisation Sessions

🎯
GoalTie everything together. You have the boxes (Ch1–3), the musical phrasing (Ch4), the licks (Ch5), and chord-tone targeting (Ch6). Now you learn to play long-form, in the moment, without stopping - real improvisation over real backing tracks.

Up to now every exercise had a "right answer" - play this lick, land on this note. Improvisation is different. It's a skill of nerve and flow, not knowledge. You already know enough notes to solo for an hour. What you're building now is the ability to keep going, to recover from mistakes, and to make musical decisions in real time. This chapter is mostly structured practice sessions rather than diagrams - because improvising is a thing you do, not a thing you read.

The mindset shift: a beginner improviser is afraid of wrong notes. An experienced one knows there are no wrong notes - only notes you leave too early. If you land somewhere that sounds off, slide or bend a fret to a chord tone and it instantly sounds intentional. The audience can't hear your plan; they only hear how you handle each note. Confidence is the technique.

▶ Watch on YouTube
📺 How to Improvise a Guitar Solo (beginner-friendly)

The Mechanics - How a Solo Is Built in Real Time

A long improvised solo is not one continuous stream of notes. It's built from phrases - short musical sentences with a beginning, a peak, and an end - separated by space (rests). Your job moment to moment is just: play a phrase, breathe, play another phrase that answers it. That's it. String enough call-and-response phrases together and you have a solo. The whole thing runs on three controls you already own:

  • Where - which box / position you're sitting in (Ch1–3).
  • What - which licks and chord tones you reach for (Ch5–6).
  • How - the phrasing: rhythm, space, bends, vibrato, dynamics (Ch4).

Improvising is just steering those three in real time. Beginners over-focus on "what." The magic is almost entirely in "how."

Theory Corner - The safety net. Over a blues or rock song in a given key, the minor pentatonic box of that key is your safe zone - every note in it will sound at least OK over every chord. That's why it's the world's most-used soloing scale: it removes the fear. Start every session inside the safe box. As you get comfortable you'll start aiming within it (chord tones from Ch6) and stepping outside it for color (the blues note, Ch4). But the box is always home base you can fall back to.

The Three-Phrase Loop - Your Improv Engine

Here's the simplest reliable structure for improvising. Repeat this loop for as long as you want to solo:

  1. Call - play a short phrase (3–5 notes). End it on a held note.
  2. Space - stop. Let it breathe for a beat or two. (This is the hardest part for beginners - silence feels wrong but it's what makes the next phrase land.)
  3. Response - play a phrase that "answers" the first - similar rhythm, but ending more resolved (often on the root).

That's one musical sentence. Then start again with a new call. Below is a written example of one call-and-response pair in A minor pentatonic so you can hear the shape of it; then you'll improvise your own.

Example: A Call-and-Response Pair (A minor pentatonic, Box 1)

The CALL - play in order: G string fret 7 → G string fret 5 → D string fret 7. Let that last note ring. (Numbers in dots = play order.)

The RESPONSE - same rhythm, but resolve home: D string fret 7 → D string fret 5 → low E fret 5 (the A root). It "answers" the call and feels finished.

Hear it: tap the CALL, count "two beats of silence" in your head, then tap the RESPONSE. That little gap is what turns two licks into a conversation. Now make up your own call, leave the gap, and answer it. You're improvising.

🗣️ Going Deeper: The Art of Call & Response

Call-and-response is the oldest idea in music - it's a field holler answered by the crowd, a preacher answered by the congregation, a singer answered by the guitar. Every great solo you love is built from it. The "call" asks a musical question; the "response" answers it. Master this one idea and you'll never noodle aimlessly again, because you'll always be doing one of two things: asking or answering.

The secret is that a response isn't a random second phrase - it has a relationship to the call. There are four main relationships, and a great soloist mixes all four. Below, each one is shown as a playable pair in A minor pentatonic Box 1 (frets 5–8) so the only thing changing is the idea, not the position. Numbers in the dots = play order.

🎵 The four kinds of response:
1. The Echo - answer with the same phrase, but end it more resolved (on the root). Comforting, song-like.
2. The Rhythmic Echo - keep the rhythm of the call but use different notes. Familiar yet fresh.
3. Question & Answer - the call ends "up in the air" on an unresolved note; the response brings it home to the root. The most satisfying.
4. Develop & Extend - repeat the call, then add a tail that takes it somewhere new. How solos grow and build.

Pair 1 - The Echo (answer it the same, but resolve)

CALL: D string fret 7 (A) → G string fret 5 (C) → G string fret 7 (D). Ends on D - sounds unfinished, hanging.

RESPONSE: the same shape, one notch lower to land home: G string fret 5 (C) → D string fret 7 (A) → low E fret 5 (A root). The ear hears "the same idea, but finished."

Pair 2 - The Rhythmic Echo (same rhythm, new notes)

CALL: a quick triplet feel - B string fret 5 (E) → B string fret 8 (G) → high E fret 5 (A). Three notes, snappy.

RESPONSE: the identical rhythm down low - D string fret 5 (G) → A string fret 7 (E) → A string fret 5 (D). Same "shape" in time, completely different pitches. Familiar but fresh.

Pair 3 - Question & Answer (hang it, then resolve)

CALL (the question): low E fret 5 (A) → A string fret 5 (D) → A string fret 7 (E). Stops on E, the tense 5th - it begs for an answer.

RESPONSE (the answer): D string fret 7 (A) → G string fret 5 (C) → D string fret 5 (G) → low E fret 5 (A root). Walks down and lands firmly home. Deeply satisfying - this is the bread and butter of blues.

Pair 4 - Develop & Extend (repeat, then add a tail)

CALL: a tiny 2-note hook - G string fret 5 (C) → G string fret 7 (D). Small and memorable.

RESPONSE: play the hook again, then extend it - G string fret 5 (C) → G string fret 7 (D) → B string fret 5 (E) → B string fret 8 (G) → high E fret 5 (A). The repeat makes it familiar; the tail makes it grow. This is exactly how a solo "builds."

The blues color trick: drop a quick passing note between C and D (the "blue note", D string fret 6 = E♭) inside any call or response and it instantly sounds bluesy. Use it as a fast slide-through, never a landing note - it's seasoning, not a home base.

Pair 5 - Add the Blue Note (the bluesy question)

CALL: G string fret 5 (C) → G string fret 6 (the blue note, slide through) → G string fret 7 (D). That middle note gives it the moan. Don't stop on it.

RESPONSE: clean and resolved - D string fret 7 (A) → D string fret 5 (G) → low E fret 5 (A root). The contrast between the gritty call and the clean answer is the whole point.

Pair 6 - The Bend Is the Answer

CALL: a plain statement - high E fret 5 (A) → B string fret 8 (G) → B string fret 5 (E). No effects, just notes.

RESPONSE: answer with feeling - G string fret 7 (D), bend it a whole step up to E, hold with vibrato, then release and land on D string fret 7 (A root). The bend turns a sentence into a cry. Technique itself can be the answer.

🎵 Theory Corner - Trading Fours. When two musicians improvise together they often "trade fours" - one plays a 4-bar phrase (the call), the other answers with their own 4 bars (the response), back and forth. You can do this alone: play a 4-bar call, then answer your own call for 4 bars, as if you're two players. It forces real listening and is the fastest way to make your phrasing sound like a conversation instead of a monologue. Many famous solos are literally the guitarist trading fours with themselves.

Call-and-Response Exercises

CR1Play All Six Pairs

Work through Pairs 1–6 above slowly. For each, play the call, count two beats of silence out loud, then play the response. Do each pair 4 times until the "shape" of each kind of answer is in your ear, not just your eyes.

CR2Same Call, Four Answers

Pick ONE call (try Pair 3's question). Now answer it four different ways: as an echo, as a rhythmic echo, as a question-and-answer resolution, and as a develop-and-extend. Same question, four different answers. This is the core creative skill - one idea has many endings.

CR3Trade Fours With Yourself (backing track)

Over a slow A minor or 12-bar blues track, play a 4-bar call, then a 4-bar response, alternating for the whole track. Make the response clearly relate to the call - echo its rhythm, or resolve its hanging note. Record it and listen for the conversation.

CR4The Vocal Test

Sing a short phrase (anything - gibberish is fine), then find it on the guitar as your call. Then sing an answer and play that as your response. Your voice naturally phrases with space and resolution - copying it onto the guitar is the single fastest way to make your solos sound human.

🎸 The Same Conversation, Up at the BB King Box

Everything you just learned works anywhere on the neck - the four kinds of answer are ideas, not finger positions. To prove it (and to get you out of fret 5), here are six more call-and-response pairs played up in Box 4, the BB King box (frets 12–15). This is the most vocal, bendable part of the neck - where blues lives - so the same conversational moves sound even more expressive here. Same notes (A minor pentatonic), one octave up. Numbers in the dots = play order.

Why up here: the strings are looser at fret 12+, so bends and vibrato come easier and sound bigger. Call-and-response plus the BB King box is the exact recipe behind a thousand famous blues solos. Loop an A minor or A blues backing track and run these.

Box 4 Pair 1 - The Echo

CALL: D string fret 14 (E) → G string fret 12 (G) → G string fret 14 (A). Hangs, unfinished.

RESPONSE: same shape, resolved home - G string fret 12 (G) → D string fret 14 (E) → A string fret 12 (A root). "Same idea, but finished."

Box 4 Pair 2 - The Rhythmic Echo

CALL: a snappy three - B string fret 13 (C) → B string fret 15 (D) → high E fret 12 (E).

RESPONSE: identical rhythm, lower notes - D string fret 12 (D) → D string fret 14 (E) → A string fret 12 (A). Same shape in time, new pitches.

Box 4 Pair 3 - Question & Answer (the blues bread-and-butter)

CALL (question): low E fret 12 (E) → A string fret 12 (A) → A string fret 15 (C). Stops up in the air, begging for an answer.

RESPONSE (answer): B string fret 15 (D) → B string fret 13 (C) → G string fret 12 (G) → A string fret 12 (A root). Walks down and lands home.

Box 4 Pair 4 - Develop & Extend

CALL: a 2-note hook - B string fret 13 (C) → B string fret 15 (D). Small, memorable.

RESPONSE: repeat the hook, then extend up - B string fret 13 (C) → B string fret 15 (D) → high E fret 12 (E) → high E fret 15 (G) → high E fret 12 (E). The repeat is the familiarity; the tail is the build.

Box 4 Pair 5 - The Signature BB Bend as the Answer

CALL: a plain statement - high E fret 12 (E) → B string fret 15 (D) → B string fret 13 (C).

RESPONSE: the famous move - G string fret 14 (A), bend a whole step up to B, hold with vibrato, release, then land on B string fret 13 (C). This is THE BB King answer. Feeling, not notes.

Box 4 Pair 6 - Octave Conversation (the two boxes talk)

CALL (down low, Box 1 region): A string fret 12 region root feel - D string fret 14 (E) → D string fret 12 (D) → A string fret 12 (A). Stated low.

RESPONSE (up high): answer the same idea an octave up on the top strings - high E fret 12 (E) → B string fret 15 (D) → high E fret 12 (E) → high E fret 15 (G). Calling low and answering high is one of the most dramatic conversation tricks there is.

🎵 Theory Corner - same idea, two voices. Notice Pair 6: the call and response are the same musical thought in two registers. Low feels like a big voice; high feels like a bright one. Moving a phrase between octaves is how a single guitar can sound like two players answering each other - and it's the easiest way to add drama to a solo without learning a single new note. You already know both boxes (Ch1–3); now you're using them as two characters in a dialogue.
CR5Run the Box 4 Pairs Over a Blues

Loop a slow A blues or A minor track and play all six Box 4 pairs with the call–space–response timing. Lean into the bends and vibrato - up here they sing. Then mix Box 1 and Box 4 pairs in the same solo, calling in one box and answering in the other.

What to Listen For

When you record yourself improvising (and you should - phone is fine), listen back for these, in order of importance:

  • Is there space? Beginners cram. If it sounds breathless, you need more rests. Space is the #1 fix.
  • Do phrases answer each other, or is it random noodling? Aim for conversation, not a run-on sentence.
  • Are you landing on strong notes at the end of phrases (root, or a chord tone from Ch6)? Endings matter more than middles.
  • Is there dynamic contrast - some quiet phrases, some loud, a build toward a peak? A solo that's all one volume is boring even if every note is "right."

Structured Sessions

Do these in order. Each one adds a constraint - constraints are what make you creative. Use a backing track (search YouTube for "A minor blues backing track 12 bar" or "A minor jam track slow").

1One-String Solo (5 min)

Improvise using ONLY the high E string (or any single string). You're forced to use rhythm, space, bends, and slides for expression instead of hiding behind lots of notes. This is the single best exercise for phrasing - it's impossible to noodle on one string.

If you can make one string sound musical, six strings is easy.

2Three-Note Solo (5 min)

Pick just THREE notes from the box (e.g. the A root, the blues note, and one more). Solo using only those three for the whole track. Forces you to discover how much music lives in rhythm and phrasing rather than in note choice.

3Call-and-Response with Yourself (10 min)

Run the Three-Phrase Loop deliberately over a slow backing track. Call - space - response. Count the silence out loud at first ("one... two..."). Every phrase must end on a held note, never trail off into mush.

4The Build (10 min)

Structure a full solo with an arc: start LOW on the neck, quiet and sparse. Over the track, gradually move higher, play more notes, get louder, until you peak with a high bend around the climax - then come back down and resolve to the root. A beginning, a middle, an end. Record it.

5Recovery Drill (10 min)

This builds the nerve. Improvise freely - and every time you hit a note that sounds wrong, DON'T stop. Slide or bend one fret to the nearest box note and hold it like you meant it. The goal is to never break the flow. By the end, wrong notes stop scaring you because you've proven you can always recover.

6Chord-Chasing Session (15 min)

Bring in Chapter 6. Over a slow 3-chord track (Am–Dm–E7 or a 12-bar blues), aim to land on a chord tone of whatever chord is currently playing at the start of each phrase. Box stays the same; your targets change with the chords. This is the difference between "playing scales over a song" and "playing the song."

Practice habit: end every practice day with ONE improv session over a backing track - even just 5 minutes. It's the most fun part and it quietly cements everything else you learned that day. Improvisation is a muscle; it only grows by reps. This is exactly the kind of focused, deliberate practice that fills the 10,000-hour timer up top with hours that actually count.

Songs to Improvise Over

  • Any 12-bar blues in A or E - the universal training ground. Endless free backing tracks online.
  • "Sunshine of Your Love" jam - Cream: classic rock improv vehicle in D.
  • "Mary Had a Little Lamb" - SRV blues version: slow blues in C, very forgiving.
  • "Hoochie Coochie Man" groove: single-chord vamp - perfect for staying in one box and focusing purely on phrasing.
✓Chapter 7 Checklist













That's the full lead-guitar arc of Book Two. From here it's reps and songs - head to the jam tracks and start applying it all to real music. El Gato, you can solo now. 🐈🎸

🎵
Miles Davis
Jazz Trumpet - revolutionized improv
⚡ Did You Know?

Davis told his musicians: 'Don't play what's there, play what's not there.' Improvisation isn't about filling space - it's about choosing silence as carefully as you choose notes. Every guitarist can steal this concept.

🎸
Rudy Ayoub
Guitarist & Educator - Musician Mansion S2
⚡ Did You Know?

Rudy Ayoub is one of the rare online guitarists who can explain music theory in a way that makes you want to practice immediately. His teaching style on Musician Mansion S2 cut through the noise.

▶ Quick Workout: The 30-Second Solo

Set a timer for 30 seconds. Pick a key (A minor is your home base). Start the timer and improvise - no stopping, no second-guessing, just play. When the timer goes off, stop immediately wherever you are.

Listen back if you recorded it, or just reflect: what was the best 2-second moment in that 30? That moment is your lick in progress. Find it on the neck, play it again deliberately, and memorize it. Every great player's vocabulary is built from happy accidents they chose to keep.

Do 3 rounds. By round 3, you'll be less self-conscious and more musical. The timer removes perfectionism.

← PreviousNext →
Book Two · Chapter 8 -- Intervals
🎵 TRACK Extended Power Chords 1
0:00

Intervals - The Building Blocks of Everything

🎯
GoalRecognize, name, and find every interval on the fretboard so you can build any scale, chord, or melody from first principles.

Everything in music - every scale, every chord, every melody - is made of intervals. An interval is simply the distance between two notes. Once you understand intervals, you stop memorizing shapes in isolation and start understanding why they work. You can derive any chord or scale from scratch, anywhere on the neck.

1. What Is an Interval?

Count the letters between two notes (including both endpoints) to get the interval number. C to E = C, D, E = 3 letters = a third. C to G = C, D, E, F, G = 5 letters = a fifth. The quality (major, minor, perfect, augmented, diminished) describes the exact number of semitones.

The 12 intervals from any root note:
Semitones Name Symbol Example from C
0Unison1C
1Minor 2ndb2C#/Db
2Major 2nd2D
3Minor 3rdb3Eb
4Major 3rd3E
5Perfect 4th4F
6Tritoneb5 / #4F#/Gb
7Perfect 5th5G
8Minor 6thb6Ab
9Major 6th6A
10Minor 7thb7Bb
11Major 7th7B
12Octave8C (high)

2. Intervals on the Guitar

Every chord formula and scale formula is written in intervals. Major chord = 1, 3, 5. Minor chord = 1, b3, 5. Major scale = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7. Pentatonic minor = 1, b3, 4, 5, b7. Once you know intervals you can build anything.

On the guitar, intervals have fixed shapes that move with you. A perfect 5th on adjacent strings (except G-B) is always 2 frets higher on the next string. A major 3rd on the same string is always 4 frets up. Learn the shapes once, use them everywhere.

3. The Most Important Intervals to Know Cold

You do not need to memorize all 12 equally. Focus on these first - they define nearly all the harmony you will ever play:

Perfect 5th (7 semitones) - power chord core

Major 3rd (4 semitones) - makes it sound happy

Minor 3rd (3 semitones) - makes it sound sad/dark

Octave (12 semitones) - same note, higher register

Tritone (6 semitones) - maximum tension/dissonance

Perfect 4th (5 semitones) - open, suspended sound

4. Intervals Build Chords

Stack intervals on top of each other and you get chords. A major chord is a major 3rd plus a minor 3rd stacked (4 semitones + 3 semitones = 7 semitones total = root, major 3rd, perfect 5th). A minor chord is a minor 3rd plus a major 3rd (3 + 4). That single interval swap - major 3rd vs minor 3rd - is the entire difference between happy and sad.

Every chord type as stacked thirds:
Major: major 3rd + minor 3rd (4 + 3)
Minor: minor 3rd + major 3rd (3 + 4)
Maj7: major 3rd + minor 3rd + major 3rd (4 + 3 + 4)
Dom7: major 3rd + minor 3rd + minor 3rd (4 + 3 + 3)
Min7: minor 3rd + major 3rd + minor 3rd (3 + 4 + 3)
Diminished: minor 3rd + minor 3rd (3 + 3)

5. Intervals Build Scales

Every scale is a specific sequence of intervals from the root. The major scale is always: W W H W W W H (whole step, whole step, half step...). The natural minor scale is: W H W W H W W. You do not need to memorize these as fret patterns - derive them from the interval formula and they will make sense anywhere on the neck.

6. Ear Training with Intervals

The most powerful use of interval knowledge is ear training. Each interval has a distinctive sound. Once you internalize these sounds you can identify any melody or chord change by ear.

Songs to anchor each interval:
Minor 2nd (b2): "Jaws" theme
Major 2nd (2): "Happy Birthday" (first two notes)
Minor 3rd (b3): "Smoke on the Water" main riff (first two notes)
Major 3rd (3): "When the Saints Go Marching In"
Perfect 4th (4): "Here Comes the Bride"
Tritone (b5): "The Simpsons" theme
Perfect 5th (5): "Star Wars" theme
Minor 6th (b6): "The Entertainer" (ascending)
Major 6th (6): "My Bonnie Lies Over the Ocean"
Minor 7th (b7): "Somewhere" from West Side Story
Major 7th (7): "Take On Me" (first two notes)
Octave (8): "Somewhere Over the Rainbow"

Exercises

1Interval Shapes Drill

On the Low E string, play A (fret 5). Then find each of the 12 intervals above it - on the same string. Say the interval name aloud as you play it. Then move to the A string and repeat. Learn the fret distances by feel.

2Build a Chord from Intervals

Starting from the note G on the Low E string (fret 3), build a G major chord using only the interval formula (1, 3, 5). Find the major 3rd (4 frets up = B) and perfect 5th (7 frets up = D) on adjacent strings. You just derived a chord without memorizing a shape.

3Interval Ear Training

Have someone play two notes on the guitar (or use a piano app). Identify the interval by sound alone before seeing the frets. Use the song anchors from the panel above as reference points. Start with just perfect 5th vs major 3rd vs minor 3rd - the three most common.

4Decode a Scale

Play the pentatonic minor scale you know. Between each pair of adjacent notes, count the semitones. Write down the interval sequence. You just derived the scale formula from the shape rather than the other way around. Now you understand why the shape is what it is.

✓Chapter 16 Checklist





You have completed the Book Two theory sequence. The tools are yours now - use them.

← PreviousNext →
Book Two · Chapter 9 -- Keys, Modes & Scales
🎵 TRACK Fingerstyle: G-C-Am-D7
0:00

Keys, Modes & Scales -- Complete Guide

▶ Watch on YouTube
🗝️
GoalUnderstand why notes sound the way they do. Learn how the major scale builds every key, where the 7 chords of a key come from, and how all seven modes are just that one scale started from a different door. This is the map under everything you already play.

Up to now you've played by shapes and by feel - pentatonic boxes, licks, chords your fingers know. That gets you a long way. But there's a layer underneath all of it, and once you see it, the whole fretboard stops being a grid of random dots and becomes a logical place where notes belong together. That layer is built from one thing: the major scale. Master this chapter and you'll know which notes fit a song before you play a single one, why a lick works over one chord but not the next, and how to make a melody sound bright, dark, jazzy, or haunting on purpose. We'll build it in order - scale, then keys, then chords, then modes - because each step is the foundation for the next. Don't skip ahead. The modes only make sense once keys click.

1. The Major Scale: the parent of everything

Every key, every mode, almost every melody in Western music comes from the major scale. It's just seven notes in a specific pattern of steps. A half step = one fret. A whole step = two frets. The major scale formula, from any starting note, is:

W   W   H   W   W   W   H

Whole, Whole, Half, Whole, Whole, Whole, Half. Start on C and follow that pattern and you get C D E F G A B - the white keys on a piano, no sharps or flats. Start on G and follow the same pattern and you get G A B C D E F# - one sharp. The pattern is always identical; only the starting note changes. That's the whole trick.

The G major scale played up the neck in open position, low to high. The numbers are the play order. Say each note name out loud as you play it: G – A – B – C – D – E – F# – G. Hearing the scale resolve back home to G is the sound of "major."

🎵 Theory Corner: scale degrees are the secret language. Musicians number the notes of a scale 1 through 7 (the "degrees"). In G major: G=1, A=2, B=3, C=4, D=5, E=6, F#=7. Once you think in degrees instead of note names, everything transfers between keys instantly - "the 5 chord" means the same thing in every key. The pentatonic boxes you already know are just degrees 1-2-3-5-6 of the major scale (major pentatonic) or 1-b3-4-5-b7 (minor pentatonic). You've been playing pieces of this scale all along.

2. What a Key Is

A key is simply "which major scale a song lives in" - the pool of seven notes the song draws from, with one note (the tonic, degree 1) that feels like home. A song "in the key of G" uses the notes of G major and keeps gravitating back to G. That's why it sounds resolved when it lands on G and unfinished when it stops anywhere else.

This is the single most useful practical payoff in this whole book: if you know the key, you know which notes will sound good. Song in G major? The G major scale (and its G major / E minor pentatonic) is your safe playground for soloing. You stop guessing and start choosing.

How to find the key fast. Look at the chords. If a song uses G, C, D, and Em, those are all chords from G major - you're almost certainly in G. The chord the song starts and ends on is usually (not always) the key. Match the chords to the chord families in the next section and the key reveals itself.

3. The 7 Chords That Live in Every Key

Here's where scales become songs. Stack three alternating notes of the major scale (a "triad") starting on each degree, and you get the seven chords that naturally belong to that key. They always come in the same pattern of major and minor:

I   ii   iii   IV   V   vi   vii°

major  ·  minor  ·  minor  ·  major  ·  major  ·  minor  ·  diminished

Big-letter Roman numerals are major, small ones are minor, and the little circle is diminished. In the key of G major that gives you:

I = G  |  ii = Am  |  iii = Bm  |  IV = C  |  V = D  |  vi = Em  |  vii° = F#dim

Suddenly the most common progressions make sense. The I–IV–V (G–C–D) you've been playing? Those are degrees 1, 4, and 5 of the key. The I–V–vi–IV "four chords of pop" (G–D–Em–C) is just a path through the family. Every song that "sounds like it goes together" does so because the chords share the same parent scale.

🎵 Theory Corner: relative minor, demystified. The vi chord (Em in G major) is the relative minor - it uses the exact same seven notes as the major key but treats degree 6 as home instead of degree 1. That's why G major and E minor are "the same notes, different center," and why your E minor pentatonic works beautifully over a G major song. A key and its relative minor are two doors into the same room.

4. The Modes: one scale, seven doors

Now the payoff. A mode is what you get when you play the major scale but treat a different degree as home. Same seven notes, different starting point, completely different mood. Play C major from C and it's bright and resolved - that's Ionian (the major scale itself). Play those same white-key notes but center everything on D, and it sounds minor-ish but hopeful - that's D Dorian. Same notes. Different home. Different feeling.

One position of the C major scale up the neck. Every mode lives inside this single shape - you change the mode by changing which note you treat as "home" and resolve to, not by moving your hand. Play it from the lowest note up and back down to feel the parent major sound first.

Here are all seven, in order, each starting on the next degree of the parent major scale. Think of them on a brightness dial - from brightest (Lydian) to darkest (Locrian):

1. Ionian (the major scale) - bright, happy, resolved. Built on degree 1. The default "major" sound. Feel: a sunny pop song.

2. Dorian - minor, but with a hopeful, sophisticated lift (a raised 6th). Built on degree 2. Feel: Santana, funk, "So What." The coolest minor.

3. Phrygian - dark, Spanish, tense (a flat 2nd right above home). Built on degree 3. Feel: flamenco, metal.

4. Lydian - major but dreamy and floating (a raised 4th). Built on degree 4. Feel: film scores, wonder, "The Simpsons" theme.

5. Mixolydian - major but bluesy and dominant (a flat 7th). Built on degree 5. Feel: rock, blues, "Sweet Home Alabama." Your go-to over dominant 7 chords.

6. Aeolian (the natural minor scale) - sad, serious, the standard minor. Built on degree 6. Feel: most minor-key songs.

7. Locrian - unstable, dissonant, rarely used as a home base (a flat 2nd AND flat 5th). Built on degree 7. Feel: tension, jazz passing color, metal.

Here is each mode as its own one-octave shape, all drawn from the same C major note pool - only the home note (and therefore the sound) changes. Brightest at the top, darkest at the bottom:

C Ionian - the major scale. Home = C. C D E F G A B. Bright and resolved.

F Lydian - the brightest mode. Home = F. Watch the B natural - that raised 4th is the dreamy, floating Lydian color.

G Mixolydian - major but bluesy. Home = G. The F natural (flat 7) is the signature note. Your go-to over dominant 7 chords.

D Dorian - the coolest minor. Home = D. The B natural (raised 6th) makes it hopeful instead of sad.

A Aeolian - the natural minor scale. Home = A. The standard "sad/serious" minor sound. You already know this one.

E Phrygian - dark and Spanish. Home = E. The F natural (flat 2nd) sitting right above the root is the tense, flamenco/metal color.

B Locrian - the darkest, most unstable mode. Home = B. Both a flat 2nd (C) and a flat 5th (F) - rarely a home base, mostly tension color.

The shortcut that makes modes click. Don't memorize seven new shapes. You already know one shape: the major scale. To play any mode, play that same parent major scale but start, land, and resolve on the mode's home note over a backing chord that emphasizes it. D Dorian = C major notes over a Dm vamp. G Mixolydian = C major notes over a G7 vamp. The notes don't move - your sense of "home" does. That's the entire secret, and it's why this chapter put keys first.

🎵 Theory Corner: the two modes you'll actually use most. Don't try to use all seven right away. In real playing, Mixolydian (for bluesy, dominant, rock contexts) and Dorian (for cool, hopeful minor grooves) carry the vast majority of the load beyond plain major and minor. Get those two into your ears and hands first - Lydian, Phrygian, and Locrian are color you reach for occasionally. Aeolian and Ionian you already know as plain minor and major.

What to Listen For

Modes are an ear skill, not a finger skill - the notes are the ones you already know. Train these:

The home note's pull. Play the parent scale, then stop on different notes. Notice how only one note per mode feels truly "at rest." That resting feeling is the mode's identity.

The one note that colors each mode. Dorian's bright 6th, Mixolydian's flat 7th, Phrygian's flat 2nd, Lydian's sharp 4th. Each mode has a signature note that gives it its flavor - lean on that note and the mode reveals itself.

Major vs. minor family. First just hear whether a mode feels major-ish (Ionian, Lydian, Mixolydian) or minor-ish (Dorian, Phrygian, Aeolian, Locrian). That coarse sort is more useful than naming all seven.

Resolution against a drone. Hold one bass note and play the scale over it - the single fastest way to actually hear a mode instead of just thinking about it.

Exercises

1Build a major scale from any note

Pick a starting note on the low E string. Apply W–W–H–W–W–W–H (whole = 2 frets, half = 1) one string at a time and say each note name out loud. Do it for G, then C, then A. Goal: build the scale from the formula without looking anything up. This wires the pattern into your understanding, not just your fingers.

2Name the 7 chords of a key

Using the I–ii–iii–IV–V–vi–vii° pattern, write out the seven chords for the key of G, then C, then D. Then take three songs you know and figure out their key by matching the chords to a family. When the chords suddenly "explain themselves," it's working.

3The drone test (hear the modes)

Find a free "drone note" track online (search "D drone" etc.) or just let a low open string ring. Play the parent major scale over it, resolving to the drone note. Move the drone to different notes of the scale and play the same notes resolving to the new home each time. You're hearing Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian… with zero new finger work. This is the exercise that makes modes real.

4Mixolydian over a dominant vamp

Vamp on a G7 (or loop a G7 backing track). Solo with the C major scale shape but make G your home note and lean on the F natural (the flat 7). That F-over-G is the bluesy Mixolydian sound. Compare it to resolving to C (plain major) and hear the difference the home note makes.

5Dorian over a minor groove

Loop a Dm chord. Solo with the same C major notes but make D home and lean on the B natural (Dorian's bright raised 6th). Notice it sounds minor but hopeful, not sad - that's the Dorian flavor. This and Exercise 4 are the two modes worth owning first.

6Key-spotting in the wild

For one week, every time you learn or hear a song, figure out its key and name its I, IV, V, and vi chords. After a week of this you'll start hearing keys automatically - and choosing the right scale to solo with becomes second nature instead of guesswork.

Where This Shows Up

Soloing: knowing the key tells you instantly which scale is safe (see Chapter 6, Soloing Over Changes). Modes let you target a specific color over each chord instead of one-size-fits-all.

Songwriting: the 7-chord family is your palette. Borrowing from it (and occasionally outside it) is how progressions are built.

The Music Theory chapter (Book One): this chapter is the natural next step up from "The Why" - revisit it and notice how much more of it now clicks.

Study Diagrams -- All Keys, Scales & Modes

The theory above in visual form. Come back here whenever you need to see a scale shape rather than think about it. Every diagram is interactive.

1. Major Scales - Six Common Keys

These are the six keys you'll encounter most on guitar. Each one is the open-position shape (most include open strings). The numbers are play order - start at 1 and climb to the top, then back down. Tap to hear the scale.

E Major

E F# G# A B C# D# E - lots of open strings, one of the richest-sounding major scales on guitar. Home key of so much classic rock.

A Major

A B C# D E F# G# A - open position. Country, rock, blues. The A chord shape you know is the I chord of this scale.

D Major

D E F# G A B C# D - starts on the A string open, works well in the lower register. Lots of folk and country songs call this home.

G Major

G A B C D E F# G - you saw this in Chapter 8. The parent scale for the Am relative minor. Sounds bright and open.

C Major

C D E F G A B C - no sharps or flats, the "white keys" key. All 7 modes in Chapter 8 were drawn from this scale.

1b. All 12 Major Keys - Complete Reference

Every major key on the guitar neck - the 6 open-position keys above plus the 6 sharp/flat keys that live higher up the neck. These are the shapes professional guitarists internalize so they can play in any key a singer needs or a chart demands. Come back here whenever a new key comes up in a song.

C Major - no sharps or flats

C D E F G A B -- the white-key key. No sharps or flats. Parent of all 7 modes in the mode study.

G Major - 1 sharp (F#)

G A B C D E F# -- lots of open strings, rich sound. Home key of classic rock.

D Major - 2 sharps (F#, C#)

D E F# G A B C# -- open position folk, country, and rock standard.

A Major - 3 sharps (F#, C#, G#)

A B C# D E F# G# -- country, blues, rock staple. Big open-string resonance.

E Major - 4 sharps (F#, C#, G#, D#)

E F# G# A B C# D# -- the richest open-string key. Hendrix, SRV, hard rock.

B Major - 5 sharps (F#, C#, G#, D#, A#)

B C# D# E F# G# A# -- 7th position. Usually a capo key on acoustic; on electric it's all barre shapes.

F# Major - 6 sharps (all except B)

F# G# A# B C# D# E# -- enharmonic to Gb. Common in metal (drop tuning) and jazz.

F Major - 1 flat (Bb)

F G A Bb C D E -- common for horn players; on guitar needs Bb (1st fret B string). Good barre chord key.

Bb Major - 2 flats (Bb, Eb)

Bb C D Eb F G A -- jazz and soul standard. Capo 3 on G feels like Bb; or use barre shapes at 5th fret.

Eb Major - 3 flats (Bb, Eb, Ab)

Eb F G Ab Bb C D -- half-step below E. Tons of SRV (he tuned down half step). Eb barre shapes at 6th fret.

Ab Major - 4 flats (Bb, Eb, Ab, Db)

Ab Bb C Db Eb F G -- 4th position. Common in R&B and gospel. Capo 1 on G covers Ab songs.

Db Major - 5 flats (Bb, Eb, Ab, Db, Gb)

Db Eb F Gb Ab Bb C -- enharmonic to C#. Often played as C# major in rock (capo 4 on A position).

All 12 keys, zero panic. On guitar you only need to memorize the open-position shapes (C, G, D, A, E) and one moveable barre shape. Every sharp/flat key is just that barre shape slid to the right fret. The Circle of Fifths (below) tells you which fret. That's the whole system.

2. Natural Minor Scales - Two Common Keys

Natural minor = Aeolian mode. Same formula as the major scale starting from degree 6 - but with a minor root. These two minor keys cover a huge amount of rock and blues.

E Natural Minor

E F# G A B C D E - the same notes as G major, but E is home. Dark, serious. This is the scale under Em, G, D, Am progressions.

A Natural Minor

A B C D E F G A - same notes as C major, but A is home. The relative minor you've been soloing in since Chapter 9.

3. The Circle of Fifths - A Visual Key Map

Every key in Western music, arranged in a circle. Moving clockwise adds one sharp to the key signature. Moving counter-clockwise adds one flat. Keys across from each other share the fewest notes - the most harmonic "distance" apart. The inner ring shows each key's relative minor.

Outer ring = major keys. Inner ring = relative minor. Rocksmith string colors used for the most common guitar keys.

4. All 7 Modes - Side by Side

All seven modes below share the same parent C major note pool (C D E F G A B) but each starts on a different root. They're shown in the same neck position so you can see which notes change from mode to mode. Brightest at top, darkest at bottom. Tap each one to hear the characteristic sound.

🎵 The one-sentence rule for each mode:
Ionian - the major scale, bright & resolved  |  Dorian - minor but hopeful (raised 6th)  |  Phrygian - dark & Spanish (flat 2nd)  |  Lydian - major but floating (raised 4th)  |  Mixolydian - major but bluesy (flat 7th)  |  Aeolian - the natural minor scale  |  Locrian - unstable & dissonant (flat 2nd + flat 5th)

Ionian - the major scale · bright · resolved

Root = D. W W H W W W H. All major-scale notes. Home feels like sunshine landing.

Lydian - dreamy, floating · raised ♯4

Root = D. The ♯4 (G# above D root) is the Lydian color - that one note makes it sound like a film score or a dream. Compare to Ionian and hear the lift.

Mixolydian - bluesy dominant · flat ♭7

Root = D. Like major but with a flat 7 (C natural over D). The signature rock/blues mode - use over dominant 7 chords. "Sweet Home Alabama," "Norwegian Wood," countless others.

Dorian - hopeful minor · raised ♮6

Root = D. Minor, but the raised 6th (B natural over D) gives it a sophisticated lift instead of straight sadness. Santana, Carlos Santana, funk bass lines, "So What" by Miles Davis.

Aeolian - natural minor · standard sad/serious

Root = D. The standard minor scale - A minor pentatonic is a subset of this. Most rock ballads, sad songs, and power-metal anthems live here.

Phrygian - dark & Spanish · flat ♭2

Root = D. The ♭2 (E♭) sitting right above the root is the signature tension. Flamenco, metal, and Middle Eastern sounds all lean on this. Hear the tense, coiled feeling.

Locrian - most dissonant · flat ♭2 + flat ♭5

Root = D. Both a flat 2nd and flat 5th - the flat 5th (A♭ / tritone) above the root makes this unstable, rarely used as a tonal center. Color for tension and jazz-metal passing tones.

5. The Complete Pentatonic Map - All 5 Boxes in A Minor

You know Box 1. Here's the whole picture: five boxes that together cover the entire neck in A minor pentatonic. They connect end-to-end - Box 2 starts where Box 1 ends, Box 3 where Box 2 ends, and so on. Box 5 wraps back to Box 1 an octave up. Tap each to hear it and memorize where it sits.

Which box to learn next? Box 1 (fret 5) is home base - you've got that. Box 4 (fret 12) is directly an octave higher and uses the same intervals as Box 1, so it's the easiest second box to internalize. Then Box 2. Save Boxes 3 and 5 for when 1, 2, and 4 feel natural.

Box 1  frets 5–8 · home base

The box you know. A on low E fret 5. Every lick from Chapters 1–9 lives here first.

Box 2  frets 8–11 · the bright-feeling box

Starts at fret 8. Feels slightly brighter. Connect to Box 1 by sliding up - that linking move is Chapter 3's "connecting the boxes."

Box 3  frets 10–13 · upper mid-neck

Less commonly taught but important for reaching into the upper register. The A root appears on fret 12 of the low E string (octave up from open).

Box 4  frets 12–15 · high octave of Box 1

Exactly one octave above Box 1. Same interval shape, higher pitch. BB King and Slash love this register for screaming leads.

Box 5  frets 2–5 · wraps around to Box 1

Frets 2–5. Box 5 connects back to Box 1 - its top notes at fret 5 are the same as Box 1's bottom notes. This is how the neck becomes one continuous loop, not five separate islands.

🎵 The pentatonic and the modes. The A minor pentatonic is a subset of Aeolian (A natural minor) - it's Aeolian with degrees 2 and 6 removed. That's why it sounds good over so much: fewer "color" notes mean fewer chances to clash. Once you know the natural minor scale (above, section 2), you can add back those two notes (B and F in A minor) to the pentatonic for extra flavor. That's the blues scale / natural minor crossover every soloist uses.

Exercises - Using This Chapter

1Play a key a day

Each day this week, pick one of the major or minor scales above and play it 10 times up and back. Say the note names out loud. After 6 days you'll have covered all 6 keys, and your fingers will start recognizing the shapes without having to look them up here.

2Mode comparison drill

Play Ionian (Section 4) up and down. Then immediately play Mixolydian starting on the same root. Stop on the flat 7 (the note that changed) and really listen to the difference it makes. Repeat with Dorian vs. Aeolian. Training your ear to hear the difference between modes is more valuable than memorizing their formulas.

3Pentatonic box crawl

Start in Box 1. Solo for 30 seconds. Then slide into Box 2 using a connecting lick. 30 seconds. Then Box 3. The goal is a seamless journey up the neck - one continuous solo, not five separate boxes. This is Chapter 3's connecting-the-boxes idea applied directly.

4Find a key by ear

Put on any song. Try to hum a note that "feels like home" with the music. Find that note on the low E string. Then try the major scale from that note - if it sounds right, you're in that major key. If it sounds minor-ish, try the natural minor. This is active ear training, not just memorizing diagrams.

✓Chapter 10 Checklist









This reference lives here forever - come back any time you need to see or hear a scale. 🎸

🎸
Steve Vai
Guitar Virtuoso
⚡ Did You Know?

Steve Vai practiced 10 hours a day for years and transcribed entire Frank Zappa albums by ear. He submitted the transcriptions to Zappa as an audition. Zappa hired him on the spot and called him 'the little Italian virtuoso.'

🎤
Anthony Vincent
Ten Second Songs - Musician Mansion S2
⚡ Did You Know?

Anthony Vincent can flip between 20+ vocal styles in a single video - his ear for mimicry is almost supernatural. On Musician Mansion S2 he showed that versatility isn't a weakness; it's a superpower.

The Pentatonic Necklace - All 5 Boxes Connected

Every pentatonic box shares notes with the next one. The yellow lines show exactly where they connect - the last notes of one box are the first notes of the next. Learn to see these handoffs and you can glide across the whole neck instead of being stuck in one box.

How to practice the necklace: Play Box 1 up, then at the top slide along the yellow line into Box 2, continue up, slide into Box 3, and so on. Come back the same way. It sounds like one continuous scale run - because it is.

Box 1 - Root Position (frets 4-7, A minor = frets 5-8)

The most-used box. Roots on strings 6 and 4. The yellow lines show where Box 1 hands off to Box 2 at the top.

Box 2 (frets 7-10)

Picked up from Box 1's top notes. Strong blues feel. Handoff to Box 3 at the top.

Box 3 (frets 9-12)

The middle of the neck. Connects both ways - back to Box 2 below, forward to Box 4 above.

Box 4 (frets 12-15)

Up at the octave. Same shape as Box 1 but an octave higher. Handoff to Box 5.

Box 5 (frets 14-17) - Connects back to Box 1

The final box. The yellow lines at the top show how it wraps back around to Box 1 (an octave up). The necklace is complete.

Keys & Modes Explorer

Tap a key and a mode below to see that exact scale shape on the fretboard. The yellow lines connect notes across string pairs so you can see the melodic shape of each mode - not just a scatter of dots.

KEY:
MODE:
A Ionian - Major scale. Bright, happy, resolved.
▶ Quick Workout: Necklace Walk

Using the pentatonic necklace map above, start at Box 1. Play 4 notes in Box 1. Slide up one position and play 4 notes in Box 2. Continue up through all 5 boxes without stopping. Then reverse direction and walk back down.

The secret: your ears learn faster than your fingers here. Hum or sing the notes as you play them. The moment you can hear the next box before your fingers arrive, you've internalized the map. That's when the whole neck opens up.

Time yourself. First run might take 30 seconds. Goal: smooth continuous flow under 10 seconds. Not for speed - for smoothness. Each note should ring clean.

Mode Comparison Study - All 7 From the Same Root

The diagrams above showed each mode in its natural position on the C major scale. Now here is a different view: all 7 modes starting from the same root note (A, 5th fret) in the same neck window. This is the best way to see exactly which notes change from mode to mode - because the root stays fixed, only the surrounding notes shift. The one or two notes that differ between modes are the "color notes" that define each mode's personality.

How to use this section: Play each shape and resolve to the root (lowest red/marked dot). Notice the feeling. Then play the next mode and compare. The color note that makes Dorian bright, Phrygian dark, Lydian floaty - you'll hear it clearly because the root doesn't move.

Mode 1: Ionian (Major) - A root. A B C# D E F# G# A. Bright, stable, "home." This is A major. Compare every other mode against this baseline.

Mode 2: Dorian - A root. A B C D E F# G A. Minor (b3 + b7) but with a natural 6th (F#). That F# vs the F natural in Aeolian is the entire Dorian sound - hopeful, cool, Santana.

Mode 3: Phrygian - A root. A Bb C D E F G A. The flat 2nd (Bb, one fret above the root) is the defining note. That half-step tension creates the Spanish/metal darkness. Lean hard on Bb over an Am chord.

Mode 4: Lydian - A root. A B C# D# E F# G# A. Major with a raised 4th (D#). That D# instead of D is the floating, dreamy shimmer. Film score gold. Over Amaj7 chords this sounds cinematic.

Mode 5: Mixolydian - A root. A B C# D E F# G A. Major with a flat 7th (G natural instead of G#). That G is the blues-rock note - use it over A7 or any dominant chord. Hendrix, Clapton, blues-rock staple.

Mode 6: Aeolian (Natural Minor) - A root. A B C D E F G A. The standard minor. You already know A minor pentatonic - this is that plus the 2nd (B) and 6th (F). Rock, ballads, metal, virtually all "sad" music.

Mode 7: Locrian - A root. A Bb C D Eb F G A. Both the 2nd AND 5th are flat. The flat 5th (Eb instead of E) makes the tonic chord diminished - it never feels "at home." Mostly used as passing color in jazz or as a sound effect in metal.

The instant-comparison trick. Play A Ionian (A major), then immediately play A Aeolian (A natural minor). That's the same root, and you hear the major-to-minor flip directly. Now try A Dorian vs A Aeolian - they're identical except for one note (F# vs F). That one note is the entire personality difference. Once your ear catches these single-note shifts, you understand modes at a deep level.

How to Actually Use Modes When Soloing

The diagrams above show where the notes are. This section tells you what to do with them when the music is moving.

The Parent Scale: C Major

All 7 modes below share the same 7 notes as C major. The diagrams show a 2-octave run starting from the mode's root note. The root (brightest dot) is always your home - the note you resolve to, bend toward, start and end phrases on.

Mode 1: Ionian (C Major) - Start on C. Bright, resolved, "happy." This is your standard major scale. Every nursery rhyme, every pop anthem in a major key. C D E F G A B C.

Mode 2: Dorian (D) - Start on D, same notes. Minor feel but with a raised 6th (B natural instead of Bb). The "cool minor" - used in Carlos Santana, Oye Como Va, So What (Miles Davis), virtually all funk. D E F G A B C D.

Mode 3: Phrygian (E) - Start on E. Dark, Spanish, flamenco. The flat 2nd (F natural right above E) creates a dramatic tension. Metallica's "Wherever I May Roam," flamenco guitar, death metal. E F G A B C D E.

Mode 4: Lydian (F) - Start on F. Dreamy, floating, cinematic. The raised 4th (B natural instead of Bb) gives it that "unresolved" shimmer. John Williams film scores, Joe Satriani's "Flying in a Blue Dream," Steve Vai. F G A B C D E F.

Mode 5: Mixolydian (G) - Start on G. Blues-rock dominant. Major feel but with a flat 7th (F natural instead of F#). Every blues-rock song in G uses this - "Sweet Home Chicago," "La Grange," basically all of ZZ Top. G A B C D E F G.

Mode 6: Aeolian (A Natural Minor) - Start on A. This IS the natural minor scale you already know as A pentatonic's full version. Melancholic, serious, rock. Stairway to Heaven, Hotel California intro, almost all heavy rock. A B C D E F G A.

Mode 7: Locrian (B) - Start on B. Unstable, dissonant, rarely used as a tonal center. The flat 2nd AND flat 5th create constant tension that wants to resolve. More theory knowledge than practical soloing tool - but it's the reason the diminished chord sounds so tense. B C D E F G A B.

The Mood Map

Here's how to remember the emotional character of each mode - from brightest to darkest:

Lydian (F)
Dreamy, floating, cinematic. Film scores.
Ionian (C)
Bright, happy, resolved. Pop, classical.
Mixolydian (G)
Blues-rock swagger. Dominant, driving.
Dorian (D)
Cool minor. Funk, jazz-fusion, Santana.
Aeolian (A)
Classic minor. Rock, metal, ballads.
Phrygian (E)
Dark, Spanish, flamenco, metal.
Locrian (B)
Unstable, dissonant. Mostly theoretical.

How to Actually Use Modes When Soloing

The practical answer: match the mode to the chord underneath you.

  • Playing over an Am chord? - A Aeolian (A natural minor). Standard minor pentatonic plus the 2nd and 6th.
  • Playing over a Dm7 chord? - D Dorian. It has that raised 6th (B) that gives Dorian its characteristic brightness vs. standard minor.
  • Playing over a G7 or G dominant chord? - G Mixolydian. You get all the blues-rock flavor without the minor tonality.
  • Playing over an Em chord in a Spanish context? - E Phrygian. Lean hard on that F note right above E.
  • Playing over a Cmaj7 or Fmaj7#11? - C Ionian or F Lydian respectively.
The one rule that makes this practical. You don't need to think "I'm now switching to Dorian." Think instead: "this chord wants me to resolve to D - and I can use B natural here instead of Bb." The mode is just a name for where you resolved and which notes you favored. Play by ear. Name it after.

The Mode vs. Pentatonic Shortcut

For 95% of rock and blues situations, the pentatonic minor gets you there. Modes give you 2 extra notes per octave - that's where the "flavor" lives. A Dorian = A minor pentatonic + the 2nd (B) and 6th (F#). A Mixolydian = A major pentatonic + the 4th (D) and flat 7th (G). Add those extra notes as color, not as the main language.

1Mode Ear Training - The Gravity Drill

Pick up your guitar. Play A natural minor scale (A B C D E F G A). Stop on A. Feel how settled that is. Now play the same notes starting and ending on C (C D E F G A B C). Notice how C now feels like "home." That shift is Aeolian vs. Ionian in the same key. Do this with all 7 starting points. 10 minutes. Your ear will start hearing modes instead of just thinking about them.

2Dorian Drill - The Santana Test

Play a Dm7 chord (or have someone hold it). Solo using D Dorian: D E F G A B C D. The B natural is the key note - that's what separates Dorian from Aeolian. Bend up to it. Linger on it. It's the note that gives Dorian that warm, soulful sound Santana built a career on.

3Mixolydian Drill - The Blues-Rock Swagger

Hold a G dominant chord. Use the G Mixolydian scale: G A B C D E F G. That F natural (flat 7) is the blues money note. Every time you hit it while G is ringing underneath, you'll hear exactly why Hendrix and Page sounded like themselves. F natural over G major = Mixolydian swagger.

✓Chapter 11 Checklist







▶ Quick Workout: Name That Mode

You've seen all 7 mode patterns. Now test if they're sticking. Go back to the diagrams above - cover the title, play the pattern on repeat, and sing or hum the scale as you play. Which mode has that bright, floating feeling? (Lydian.) Which one sounds tense and unstable? (Locrian.) Which one feels like home? (Ionian - it is the major scale.)

For each mode, ask yourself: where is the root note on string 5? Play up from that root, say the mode name when you finish, then move to the next. You don't need to memorize all 7 today - pick two contrasting ones (Ionian + Dorian, or Lydian + Phrygian) and drill those until each one feels distinct.

Bonus: hum a simple melody while staying inside one mode's box. You'll start hearing the flavor instantly.

Chapter 12 - Chord Theory: How Chords Are Built - coming next.

✓Keys, Modes & Scales Checklist














Chapter 9 -- Chord Theory -- is next.

← PreviousNext →
Book Two · Chapter 10
🎵 TRACK Open Seventh Chords 1
0:00

Chord Theory - How Every Chord Gets Built

🎯
GoalUnderstand exactly why a chord sounds the way it does - so you can build any chord, recognize any chord by ear, and never be stuck when a chart shows something unfamiliar.

Every chord you have ever played is a stack of intervals pulled from a scale. That's it. Once you see the pattern, you can build any chord from scratch - major, minor, diminished, augmented, seventh, ninth, suspended - all of them follow the same handful of rules.

1. The Building Block: The Interval

An interval is the distance between two notes. Two notes played together are an interval. Stack three intervals and you have a chord. The ones that matter most:

Name Semitones Sound Example from C
Minor 2nd1Clashing, tenseC - C#
Major 2nd2Stepwise, neutralC - D
Minor 3rd3Sad, darkC - Eb
Major 3rd4Happy, brightC - E
Perfect 4th5Open, stableC - F
Perfect 5th7Powerful, hollowC - G
Minor 7th10Bluesy, dominantC - Bb
Major 7th11Jazzy, lushC - B

2. Triads - The Foundation of Every Chord

A triad is three notes: a root, a third, and a fifth. The type of third (major or minor) and the type of fifth (perfect, augmented, or diminished) give you the four basic triad types.

Major
Root + Major 3rd + Perfect 5th
Formula: 1 - 3 - 5
Sound: Bright, happy
Example: C - E - G
Minor
Root + Minor 3rd + Perfect 5th
Formula: 1 - b3 - 5
Sound: Sad, dark
Example: C - Eb - G
Diminished
Root + Minor 3rd + Flat 5th
Formula: 1 - b3 - b5
Sound: Tense, unstable
Example: C - Eb - Gb
Augmented
Root + Major 3rd + Sharp 5th
Formula: 1 - 3 - #5
Sound: Dreamy, unresolved
Example: C - E - G#

3. Major vs. Minor - The One Note That Changes Everything

The difference between a C major and a C minor chord is exactly one note: the 3rd. Lower the major 3rd by one fret (one semitone) and you turn any major chord into its minor. Raise the minor 3rd by one fret and you get major. That single half-step creates the difference between happy and sad.

C Major Triad

C (root) - E (maj 3rd) - G (5th)

C Minor Triad

C (root) - Eb (min 3rd) - G (5th)

4. Seventh Chords - Add One More Note

Stack one more third on top of any triad and you get a seventh chord. The four main types:

Major 7th (maj7)
1 - 3 - 5 - 7
Lush, jazzy. "Beautiful" feeling.
Cmaj7: C E G B
Dominant 7th (7)
1 - 3 - 5 - b7
Bluesy, wants to resolve.
C7: C E G Bb
Minor 7th (m7)
1 - b3 - 5 - b7
Smooth, jazzy minor. Soul music.
Cm7: C Eb G Bb
Half-Dim (m7b5)
1 - b3 - b5 - b7
Dark, tense. Jazz ii chord in minor.
Cm7b5: C Eb Gb Bb

5. Suspended Chords - No Third at All

A suspended chord replaces the third with either the 2nd or 4th. Without the 3rd there's no major or minor - the chord just hangs, waiting to resolve. This is where the word "suspended" comes from.

  • sus2: Root + 2nd + 5th - open, wide sound. Csus2 = C D G
  • sus4: Root + 4th + 5th - tense, wants to drop back to major. Csus4 = C F G

Classic sus4 moment: the opening of The Who's "Pinball Wizard." That Asus4 - A riff is pure suspended tension releasing to resolution.

6. Voice Leading - How Chords Move Smoothly

Voice leading is the art of moving from one chord to the next with minimum motion. The best progressions feel smooth because individual notes move by small steps - one or two frets - rather than jumping. Here is a practical C - Am - F - G voice leading example with common tones held where possible:

C Major

A Minor

F Major

G Major

Notice: C and Am share E and G. F and C share C. The movement from chord to chord is minimal - that smoothness is voice leading in action.

The shortcut that makes you sound advanced. On any major chord, try flattening the 3rd (move that one finger down one fret). Instant minor. On any barre chord this is trivial - one finger adjustment. This is how rock guitarists shift between major and minor voicings on the fly.
1Build Any Chord From Its Name

Take any chord name and decode it: start with the root, add a major or minor 3rd (4 or 3 semitones), add a perfect 5th (7 semitones from root). For "G minor" - G is the root, count up 3 semitones for Bb (minor 3rd), count up 7 semitones from G for D (5th). G Bb D. Find those three notes on any part of the neck. That's G minor. Do this with 5 random chords.

2The Major-to-Minor Drill

On any barre chord shape you know, find where the major 3rd lives. Move that single note down one fret. You just made a minor chord. Now do it in reverse - minor to major. Practice this at frets 3, 5, 7, and 9 using E-shape barre chords. Say the chord name out loud each time.

3Voice Leading: C - Am - F - G

Play the C - Am - F - G progression (from the diagrams above) very slowly. Watch your fingers. Which ones barely move? Which ones jump? The ones that barely move are your common tones - the glue. Try to make each transition as smooth as possible, letting common tones ring through the change instead of re-picking them.

Practice Audio - Troy Stetina

🎵 Track Seventh Barre Chords 1 (F7-Bb7)
0:00
✓Chapter 12 Checklist






▶ Quick Workout: Build It From Scratch

Pick any note on the fretboard - say, G on string 6 fret 3. Now build a major triad from scratch without looking: root (G), major 3rd (+4 semitones = B), perfect 5th (+7 semitones = D). Play those three notes individually across strings, then play them as a chord shape you know. Say each interval name as you pick the note.

Now flatten the 3rd by one fret. You've just built a G minor triad. Hear the difference. That one note - the 3rd - is everything. Major vs minor lives entirely there.

Try it again with A, then D. Three different roots, same formula. The whole theory system is just this loop repeated everywhere on the neck.

If you really want it to stick: play through the C major scale one note at a time and label each degree - R, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7. That's the map every chord is built from.

Chapter 13 - Seventh Chords on the Neck - coming next.

← PreviousNext →
Book Two · Chapter 11

Seventh Chords on the Neck - Maj7, Min7, Dom7, m7b5

🎯
GoalAdd four essential seventh chord shapes to your vocabulary - play them as both open voicings and barre chord forms, and hear exactly what each one contributes to a progression.

A seventh chord is any triad with a seventh interval stacked on top. You've already played dominant 7th chords (A7, E7, D7) from Book One. Now we go deeper: four complete shapes for each type, across the neck, with the ear-training to tell them apart.

The Four Types - Quick Reference

Major 7th (maj7)
1 - 3 - 5 - 7. Lush, resolved. Jazz, R&B ballads. The Beatles "Something."
Dominant 7th (7)
1 - 3 - 5 - b7. Tension, wants to resolve. Blues, funk, rock. "Purple Haze" chord sequence.
Minor 7th (m7)
1 - b3 - 5 - b7. Smooth, cool minor. Soul, funk, jazz. Basically all of Steely Dan.
Half-Dim (m7b5)
1 - b3 - b5 - b7. Dark, tense. The ii chord in minor-key jazz progressions.

Major 7th (maj7) - Open Voicings

The major 7th has a natural, lush quality. The 7th note sits just one half-step below the octave root, creating a sound that feels "almost home but beautifully suspended." Cmaj7 and Fmaj7 are the two most useful open-position shapes.

Cmaj7

C E G B - just lift your ring finger off a C chord

Amaj7

A C# E G# - moveable form based on A shape

Minor 7th (m7) - The Funk/Soul Workhorse

Minor 7th chords are the backbone of funk, soul, and jazz-influenced rock. Am7 and Em7 are both dead-easy from open position - and both are just their minor chord with one note changed.

Am7

A C E G - Am chord with open G string

Em7

E G B D - Em with open D string added

Dominant 7th (7) - The Blues Tension Chord

You know these already. The dominant 7th has a b7 that creates harmonic tension wanting to resolve up a 4th (A7 wants to resolve to D, E7 wants to resolve to A). This is the engine of the blues and of classical harmony both.

A7

A C# E G - one finger lift from A chord

E7

E G# B D - one finger removed from E chord

Half-Diminished (m7b5) - The Dark ii Chord

Also written as ø (the half-dim symbol). You'll encounter this as the ii chord in a minor-key ii-V-i progression - the jazz fundamental. Bm7b5 - E7 - Am is one of the most common minor-key jazz cadences in existence. It sounds ominous and inevitable.

Bm7b5

B D F A - the ii chord in A minor jazz progressions

Dm7b5
