D F Ab C - the ii chord in C minor progressions

Practice Tracks - Troy Stetina

These two tracks are seventh-chord workouts. Track 78 is F7 and Bb7 dominant seventh shapes; track 79 continues with moveable seventh barre chord patterns. Use them as backing tracks to practice voicing-switching between chord types.

The quick maj7 trick. Any major chord you know: find the 3rd (the note that makes it major) and play the note one fret BELOW it. That's the major 7th. On Cmaj7 - the note B on the 2nd string 1st string 0 is already there in the open position. On a barre chord F major: the major 7th (E) lives one fret below the root on the same string, or you can omit the root and stack just the upper three notes for a clean jazz voicing.
1The Seventh Chord Substitution Drill

Take any chord progression you know. Play it once with plain major/minor chords. Then play it again, substituting 7th chords wherever they fit: major chords become maj7, minor chords become m7, the V chord (the dominant) keeps its 7. Notice how the progression thickens and breathes differently. Try it with C - Am - F - G becoming Cmaj7 - Am7 - Fmaj7 - G7.

2Minor ii-V-i - The Jazz Cadence

Play Bm7b5 - E7 - Am, slowly. Listen to the pull from Bm7b5 (tense) to E7 (more tense) to Am (release). That is the most important three-chord sequence in jazz. Practice it in at least two keys: Bm7b5 - E7 - Am, and Dm7b5 - G7 - Cm.

CAGED Positions -- All Four Types Across the Neck

Now that you know the basic voicings, here are all five CAGED positions for each 7th chord type. This is where the neck opens up.

Why 7th Chords in All 5 Positions?

Most players know one or two Cmaj7 shapes. A complete player knows five - one anchored on each CAGED root note. The moment you're stuck in a key, that's the gap. These 20 shapes close it.

A quick refresher on the four types and what they sound like:

  • Maj7 (1-3-5-7) - Lush, romantic, jazzy. Think "Misty," classic soul ballads.
  • min7 (1-b3-5-b7) - Smooth, cool, soulful. The backbone of funk and jazz.
  • dom7 (1-3-5-b7) - Bluesy tension that wants to resolve. The workhorse of blues and rock.
  • m7b5 (1-b3-b5-b7) - Dark, diminished-flavored. The ii chord in minor keys.

1. Maj7 - Five CAGED Positions

We'll use C as the root throughout so all five shapes show the same chord in different locations. On the neck, Cmaj7 = C major + B natural on top.

Cmaj7 - C shape (fret 3)

Cmaj7 - A shape (fret 3)

Cmaj7 - G shape (fret 7)

Cmaj7 - E shape (fret 8)

Cmaj7 - D shape (fret 10)

2. min7 - Five CAGED Positions

Cm7 = Cmaj7 but with a flatted 3rd. The same five positions, slightly different fingering.

Cm7 - C shape (fret 3)

Cm7 - A shape (fret 3)

Cm7 - G shape (fret 8)

Cm7 - E shape (fret 8)

Cm7 - D shape (fret 10)

3. Dom7 - Five CAGED Positions

C7 = Cmaj7 but the 7th is flatted (Bb instead of B). That one semitone drop creates all the blues tension.

C7 - C shape (fret 3)

C7 - A shape (fret 3)

C7 - G shape (fret 8)

C7 - E shape (fret 8)

C7 - D shape (fret 10)

4. m7b5 - Five CAGED Positions

Cm7b5 (also written Cm half-diminished) = Cm7 with the 5th flatted. Dark and tense - you'll hear it as the ii chord in minor key progressions (like Bm7b5 in the key of C minor).

Cm7b5 - C shape (fret 3)

Cm7b5 - A shape (fret 3)

Cm7b5 - E shape (fret 8)

How to Practice These

1One Quality, Five Positions

Pick Maj7. Play all five Cmaj7 shapes in order, lowest to highest on the neck. Use a metronome - 4 beats per shape. Then move the whole set to G, D, A, E. One quality across all keys before moving to the next.

2ii-V-I with Position Locking

In C major: Dm7 - G7 - Cmaj7. Force yourself to stay in one CAGED region - all three chords within 4 frets of each other. Then shift to the next region. This is how jazz players think.

3Chord Quality Switch Drill

Pick one position (say, A-shape). Play Cmaj7 - Cm7 - C7 - Cm7b5, all in the same CAGED position. Four beats each. Hear how a single interval change shifts the entire mood.

✓Chapter 14 Checklist





Chapter 15 - Voice Leading - coming next.

✓Seventh Chords Checklist











Chapter 14 -- Voice Leading -- is next.

← PreviousNext →
Book Two · Chapter 13 -- Nashville Number System

Nashville Number System

Nashville session musicians read chord charts without knowing the key in advance. The Nashville Number System (NNS) makes that possible - every chord is just a number. Change the key, the numbers stay the same.

▶ Watch on YouTube

The Core Idea

Every major scale has 7 notes. Each note gets a number: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7. Any chord built on that note gets the same number. That's it.

So a chord chart written as 1 - 4 - 5 - 1 works in ANY key. The numbers tell you the relationship - you pick the key.

Numbers in Every Key

The table below shows what chord each number lands on for common keys. The pattern is always the same - major, minor, minor, major, major, minor, diminished.

Key1234567
GGAmBmCDEmF#dim
AABmC#mDEF#mG#dim
CCDmEmFGAmBdim
DDEmF#mGABmC#dim
EEF#mG#mABC#mD#dim

Columns 1 and 4 and 5 are major. Columns 2, 3, 6 are minor. Column 7 is diminished. Always.

Quality Markers

When writing NNS charts, minor chords get a dash: 2- 3- 6-. Major chords are just numbers. A 7th chord gets the number written above: 57. That's all the notation you need for 90% of songs.

1 = major
2- = minor
57 = dominant 7th
1maj7 = major 7th

The Big 3: 1 - 4 - 5

Most rock, country, and blues runs on three chords: the 1 (home), the 4 (lift), and the 5 (tension that wants to go home). Learn these three in every key you play and you can fake your way through hundreds of songs.

Key1 (Home)4 (Lift)5 (Tension)
GGCD
AADE
CCFG
DDGA
EEAB

Read a Real Chart

Here is a standard 12-bar blues written in NNS. Try playing it in G, then in A - only the chords change, not the chart.

14 beats
14 beats
14 beats
14 beats
44 beats
44 beats
14 beats
14 beats
54 beats
44 beats
14 beats
54 beats

12-bar blues - the backbone of rock, blues, and country. 1 = home, 4 = lift, 5 = turnaround.

Interactive: Pick a Key

Select a key below and see what each number maps to:

← PreviousNext →
Book Two · Chapter 12
🎵 TRACK Barre Chord Progression 1
0:00

Voice Leading - Connecting Chords Smoothly

🎯
GoalMake chord changes sound fluid and intentional by moving individual notes the shortest distance possible between chords.

There is a massive difference between two guitarists playing the same chord progression. One grabs shapes and strums. The other thinks about where each note is going. Voice leading is that second approach - it is the art of choosing chord voicings so that individual notes move smoothly from one chord to the next.

This is how jazz players make even simple I-IV-V progressions sound sophisticated. It is how classical composers wrote music that felt inevitable. And it is a skill that will immediately make your rhythm playing more musical.

1. What "Voices" Are

Think of each string as a separate voice in a choir - soprano, alto, tenor, bass. When you strum a chord you are sounding all those voices at once. Voice leading means paying attention to where each voice moves when you change chords.

The golden rule: move each voice the smallest interval possible to reach the next chord. If a note is shared between two chords, keep it in the same place (common tone). If it has to move, move it by a half step or whole step rather than a leap.

2. Common Tones - Your Best Friend

Many chords share notes. C major and A minor both contain E and C. G major and E minor both contain G and B. When you find these shared notes and keep them stationary while the other voices move, the transition feels effortless.

C major - notes: C E G

A minor - shares E and C with Cmaj

F major - shares C with Cmaj

In the C-Am-F-G progression (the most common progression in pop music), if you choose voicings that keep common tones in place you get a smooth, flowing sound. The bass might move but the inner voices barely budge.

3. Guide Tones - The 3rds and 7ths

In any chord, the most harmonically important notes are the 3rd and the 7th. These are called guide tones. They define whether the chord is major or minor, dominant or major seventh. When voice leading between chords, prioritize smooth movement in the guide tones and everything else falls into place.

ii-V-I in C major (Dm7 - G7 - Cmaj7):
The 3rd of Dm7 is F. The 7th of G7 is F. Same note - common tone, no movement needed.
The 7th of Dm7 is C. The 3rd of G7 is B. That is a half-step down - minimal movement.
The 3rd of G7 is B. The 7th of Cmaj7 is B. Common tone again.
The guide tones barely move. That is why ii-V-I sounds so resolved.

Dm7 (ii) - 3rd=F, 7th=C

G7 (V) - 3rd=B, 7th=F

Cmaj7 (I) - 3rd=E, 7th=B

4. Inversions for Smoother Bass Movement

The bass note does not have to be the root. Playing a chord with the 3rd or 5th in the bass is called an inversion. Inversions let you create a stepwise bass line - a note moving up or down by steps - which is one of the most powerful voice leading tools available.

Classic example: C - C/E - F - G. The bass moves C-E-F-G stepwise upward while the harmony changes above it. You hear this in countless classic rock and pop songs.

C major (root position) - bass: C

C/E (first inversion) - bass: E

F major - bass: F (stepwise)

5. Substitutions - Same Function, Smoother Motion

Sometimes a chord substitution exists not because it sounds "different" but because it leads more smoothly to the next chord. The most common guitar substitution: replacing G major with G/B when moving to C major. The bass steps up B-C instead of leaping G-C.

John Mayer, David Gilmour, and Keith Richards all built careers on choosing richer substitutions over plain chords.

Practice tip: Take any I-IV-V progression you know. Play it once with the basic open shapes. Then play it again using inversions and common-tone voicings. Record both versions and compare. You will hear the difference immediately.

Exercises

1Common Tone Hunt

Write out the notes in C, Am, F, and G. Circle every note that appears in more than one chord. Now play the progression and try to keep those shared notes on the same string and fret across the changes. 4 beats per chord, slow tempo.

2ii-V-I Voice Leading Drill

Play Dm7 - G7 - Cmaj7 using the diagrams above. On each change, notice which fingers stay put (common tones) and which move. Practice until the transition feels like breathing - no gaps, no hesitation.

3Walking Bass Line

Play C - C/E - F - G using the inversion diagrams. Focus on the lowest note of each chord forming a smooth ascending line. Then try to create a descending version: G - G/F - C/E - C.

4Song Application

Pick any song you already know. Find one chord change where the transition feels abrupt. Look up whether an inversion exists that creates a smoother bass movement. Replace that one chord and see if the progression flows better.

✓Chapter 15 Checklist





Chapter 16 - Intervals - coming next.

← PreviousNext →
Book Two · Chapter 14

Fretboard Key Explorer

🎵
GoalPick any key and instantly see its pentatonic boxes, major scale, and all 7 modes lit up on the full neck -- one panel that replaces bouncing between chapters.

Every scale, mode, and box position you have learned lives on the same fretboard. This panel maps them all at once. Pick a root key, pick what you want to see, and watch the neck light up in Rocksmith colors. Use it as a reference while you practice, not a thing to memorize in one sitting.

Key
Show
Mode

How to use this

Select a key at the top -- the root note turns gold on every string. Then choose a view: Pentatonic shows all 5 box positions color-coded by box number. Major Scale shows the full 7-note pattern across the neck. Mode lets you pick any of the 7 modes and highlights its unique intervals. The note dots are colored by string (your Rocksmith colors) so you can read them against your muscle memory.

1Root anchor drill

Pick a key. Find all the gold root dots on the neck. Play just those -- low E to high E. You are anchoring the key in your ear and fingers before adding any other notes.

2Box-to-box flow

Switch to Pentatonic view. Play Box 1, then find where Box 2 starts and flow into it without stopping. Keep going through all 5. This is the exercise that makes the neck feel like one connected thing instead of five separate shapes.

3Mode color comparison

Set the key to A. Switch the view to Mode and cycle through Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian. Listen to how the character changes even though many notes overlap. The blue (4th-string) notes shift most noticeably -- your ear will start to hear the difference before your brain explains it.

← PreviousNext →
Book Three -- Genre Masterclass

🎵 Welcome to Book Three

🎯
GoalTake everything you've learned and apply it to real genres -- heavy metal, blues, and gospel. This is where technique meets soul.

Books One and Two gave you the vocabulary. Book Three is about learning to speak in different dialects. Every genre has its own logic -- a set of moves, feels, and unwritten rules that make it immediately recognizable. You're going to learn those rules from the inside out.

Each chapter in Book Three is built around a genre's core vocabulary: the riffs that define it, the scales that power it, the rhythmic feel that drives it, and the landmark recordings you should have memorized in your ear.

What's Inside Book Three

Chapter Genre Core Skill
Ch 1 Heavy Metal Gallop rhythms, palm muting, power chord riffs
Ch 2 Blues The blues scale, string bends, slow burning feel
Ch 3 Gospel Upper-register voicings, passing chords, major feel

You don't have to go in order. If blues is your home, start there. If metal lit the fire that made you pick up the guitar, start with Chapter 1. These genres respect each other -- the bends in blues live inside gospel; the aggression of metal lives inside blues. Let them cross-pollinate.

★Before You Dive In

For each genre chapter, do this ritual before reading a single word of lesson:

1. Pull up one of the "ear anchors" listed at the top of the chapter.
2. Listen for 3 minutes with your eyes closed.
3. Then open the chapter and read.

Your brain learns guitar through both your hands and your ears. Start with your ears.

Chapter 1 -- Blues -- starts below.

← PreviousNext →
Book Three · Chapter 2
🎵 TRACK Blues Sixth Comping
0:00

🎸 Blues

🎵
GoalOwn the blues scale, master the string bend as a vocal expression tool, and learn to play in the space between notes.

Ear anchors -- listen before you read: The Thrill Is Gone (BB King), Red House (Jimi Hendrix), Texas Flood (Stevie Ray Vaughan). Three completely different players, same tradition. Feel how each one breathes.

1. The Blues Is a Conversation

The first and most important thing to understand about blues: it's not a style, it's a language. The guitar is answering a question. The bend that hangs in the air is the vocalist's hesitation before saying something hard. The quick lick after a chord change is punctuation. When BB King plays a single note and holds it, he's not decorating -- he's saying something.

Every technique in this chapter is in service of that conversation. Learn the mechanics, then learn to forget them.

2. The Blues Scale

You already know the minor pentatonic (5 notes). The blues scale adds one more: the b5 (the "blue note" or tritone). In A minor pentatonic: A - C - D - E - G. The blues scale adds Eb between D and E. That Eb is the note that aches.

A blues scale -- box 1. The blue note (b5, Eb) sits between D and E. Play it as a passing tone -- land briefly, then resolve up to E or down to D.

A blues scale -- box 2, higher up the neck. SRV and Hendrix spent most of their time in this register. Connect box 1 and box 2 seamlessly.

3. The String Bend -- Your Voice on the Guitar

The bend is what humanizes the blues. A pure fretted note is a computer's voice. A bent note is a human voice -- imprecise, emotive, capable of landing anywhere on the pitch spectrum. Master three types:

Full bend: Push the string up until the pitch rises exactly one whole step. Verify by playing the note two frets higher first -- that's your target pitch.
Half bend: Same motion, half the distance. One semitone up.
Pre-bend and release: Bend silently to pitch, pick it, then release slowly. The note "falls" rather than rises. This is the crying sound.

The quintessential BB King-style lick: play G (string 3, fret 12), bend up to A, release, land on E (string 2, fret 9). Three notes, maximum expression.

SRV-style double-stop lick. Simultaneous notes on strings 2 and 3, partial bend on the B string. Use one finger for both strings -- the bend moves both notes together.

4. The 12-Bar Blues Shuffle

The 12-bar form is the framework almost all blues is built on (you learned it in Book One). Now add shuffle feel: instead of even eighth notes, you play long-short, long-short. The ratio is roughly dotted-eighth + sixteenth, or pure triplet feel (first and third note of each triplet). The classic shuffle pattern alternates root and 5th (or 6th) on the same string pair.

E shuffle pattern. Alternate between open low E and fret 2, then fret 4 on string 5. This is the acoustic foundation of the electric blues -- shuffle the rhythm, don't play it straight.

5. Vibrato -- The Sustain That Breathes

Blues vibrato is slower and wider than rock vibrato. Hold any fretted note. Don't play vibrato immediately -- let the note ring for half a second. Then begin a slow, even oscillation from the wrist and shoulder, not just the fingers. Slow is harder than fast here. Don't rush. BB King's vibrato is his most recognizable attribute -- nobody else has that exact speed and width.

6. Classic Blues Moves to Own

The classic blues turnaround lick. Descending run from the 5th to the root with chromatic passing note. Used at the end of every 12-bar sequence to "turn" back to the top.

Hendrix-style double-stop move. Simultaneous notes on strings 2 and 3, sliding up by one step. The slide is what makes it Hendrix rather than BB King.

"Thrill Is Gone" style slow bend. Single note on the B string, full bend and hold. This is a study in note duration and vibrato control, not speed.

7. Playing in the Spaces

The hardest blues skill is restraint. Beginners fill every measure. Great blues players leave room. Rule: one strong phrase, then rest. Play for two bars, silence for two bars. Let the listener lean in. When you do play, every note must count -- velocity, duration, and pitch all matter more than note count.

This is why practicing slow blues is more valuable than practicing fast blues. At 60 BPM you can't hide behind momentum. Every note has to justify its existence.

1Call and Response Drill

Over any backing track in Am or E blues -- Rule: play exactly 2 bars, rest exactly 2 bars. Repeat for 10 minutes.

In your rest bars, listen and feel where the phrase wants to go next. Your next "call" should answer the previous one, not repeat it. When this feels natural, reduce to 1 bar on, 1 bar off. This is how BB King thinks.

2Bend Accuracy Drill

On the B string, fret 8 (G note):

Step 1: Play fret 10 (A) -- this is your target pitch for a full bend from fret 8.
Step 2: Bend fret 8 up. Match the pitch of fret 10 exactly. Use your ear, not a tuner.
Step 3: Repeat 20 times. Each bend should land on pitch. If you're going flat or sharp, adjust the amount of push.

✓Chapter 2 Checklist







Chapter 2 -- Gospel -- is next.

← PreviousNext →
Book Three · Chapter 3
🎵 TRACK Fingerpicking Style
0:00

🎙 Gospel

🎵
GoalLearn upper-register chord voicings, gospel chord substitutions, and the fills that live between the changes in gospel and R&B.

Ear anchors -- listen before you read: People Get Ready (Curtis Mayfield), Sign O' the Times (Prince), Until You Come Back to Me (Aretha Franklin). Listen for how the guitar is texture, punctuation, and chorus all at once.

1. Gospel Is the Root of Everything

You can trace a direct line from gospel to blues to soul to R&B to rock to funk. Gospel guitar is where chord voicings became voices -- where the notes in a chord weren't just harmony but feeling. Curtis Mayfield played in open F# tuning and used chord fragments high on the neck that sounded like a choir. Prince pulled gospel chord logic into pop songs and made them feel like church. When you learn gospel guitar, you're learning the vocabulary that American popular music was built on.

2. Triads High on the Neck

Gospel guitar rarely uses open-position chords. The sound comes from triads -- three-note chord shapes -- played on strings 1-2-3 or 2-3-4, high up the neck. These voicings cut through a choir, through piano, through everything. They're thin but bright, and on a clean or lightly overdriven tone, they shimmer.

E major triad -- strings 3, 2, 1 at fret 9. Root (E) on string 3, major 3rd (G#) on string 2, 5th (B) on string 1. This is the core gospel/soul voicing.

A major triad -- strings 3, 2, 1. Root (A) on top. Moving this shape across the neck gives every major triad -- fully moveable.

D major triad on strings 4, 3, 2. Different string group -- gives a slightly fuller, warmer tone. Used as a mid-register fill between vocal phrases.

3. The Gospel Chord Move -- IV-I and the Add9

The most characteristic gospel chord progression is IV to I -- swinging from the four chord down to the one. Gospel doesn't play it straight -- it adds suspended notes, major 9ths, and chromatic passing chords that create the sensation of reaching for and then landing on home.

The add9 chord (major triad + 9th, no 7th) is the core of gospel harmony. It sounds hopeful, luminous, unresolved in just the right way. The move from Gadd9 to G is the harmonic equivalent of a deep breath finally released.

Gadd9 chord. The 9th (A) is on string 2, open. This chord is everywhere in gospel, praise music, and Prince. The open string rings sympathetically against the fretted notes.

Amaj7 voicing for gospel. Major 7th chords (not dominant 7th) are the color of gospel -- bright and spiritual. The F# (major 7th of A) lifts the chord upward.

4. Passing Chords -- The Chromatic Gospel Walk

Gospel harmony moves by half steps. A chromatic passing chord is a chord one fret below your destination -- play it on the off-beat, then move up to the target chord on the downbeat. This creates momentum that the resolution pays off.

Example: going from G to C, sneak in a B chord (or Bmaj triad) on the "and" before beat 1 of the C. Play it lightly, don't linger. Then land on C with conviction. That's the gospel passing chord move.

Chromatic passing chord approach to Cmaj7. Play B major triad one fret below, then shift up to C voicing. The half-step movement creates a miniature tension-resolution arc.

5. Fills Between the Changes

Between vocal phrases and chord changes, gospel guitar plays short melodic fills -- answers. Keep them light, 2-4 notes, and always resolve to a note in the next chord. The fill is a question; the chord is the answer. Don't let the fill call attention to itself -- it should feel inevitable.

Gospel fill in G -- ascending run from G to D. Play with hammer-ons for a smooth, legato feel. The last note (D) is the 5th of G -- a stable resolution point.

Curtis Mayfield-style high register fill. Two-string double-stop on the upper strings, slid up one fret for brightness. Light touch, clean tone -- this is not a rock lick.

6. Tone and Feel for Gospel

Amp: Clean or just barely breaking up. No heavy distortion -- gospel lives in clarity.
Pickup: Neck pickup or neck-middle blend. Bridge pickup for fills only.
Dynamics: Play chord changes softer than you think. Play fills slightly louder so they punctuate.
Timing: Gospel sits slightly behind the beat -- not dragging, but settling into each note. If you're tense, the music sounds tense. Breathe.

7. Putting It Together

The G - D - Em - C progression (I - V - vi - IV) is the four-chord foundation of gospel and praise music. Practice it with these ingredients: (1) upper-register triads instead of open chords, (2) chromatic approach before each change, (3) a two-note fill after each landing, (4) light pick attack and ringing strings.

1Major Triads Across All 12 Keys

Take the E major triad shape (strings 3-2-1 at fret 9). Move it through all 12 fret positions starting at fret 1. Name each chord as you play it. When clean at 80 BPM (one chord per beat), you own the moveable major triad -- the foundation of gospel, soul, R&B, and pop rhythm guitar.

2I - IV - V Gospel Progression in A

Chords: A - D - E using upper-register triads. Before each change, briefly touch the chord one fret below. After landing, play a 2-4 note fill from the A major scale before moving on. Loop continuously for 5 minutes. The fills should feel more natural each pass through.

✓Chapter 3 Checklist







Chapter 3 -- Heavy Metal -- is next.

← PreviousNext →
Book Three · Chapter 1
🎵 TRACK Hard Rock Riff (Advanced)
0:00

⚡ Heavy Metal

🎸
GoalMaster palm-muted power chord riffs, the gallop rhythm, and the minor-pentatonic + Phrygian lead vocabulary that defines metal.

Ear anchors -- listen before you read: Master of Puppets (Metallica), Holy Wars (Megadeth), Cowboys from Hell (Pantera). These three recordings contain almost everything this chapter covers. Put them in your ears first.

1. Palm Muting -- The Engine of Metal

Palm muting is what separates a metal riff from a punk riff. Rest the heel of your picking hand lightly on the bridge, just in front of where the strings leave the saddles. Too far forward = dead thud. Too far back = open string. The sweet spot is maybe 1 cm in front of the saddle -- you want a chunky, percussive tone that still has pitch.

On a distorted amp, properly palm-muted power chords become a grinding, mechanical roar. That's the sound. Practice switching between muted (PM) and open (full ring) mid-riff -- the contrast is what makes metal riffs breathe.

2. The E5 Power Chord Riff (Master of Puppets Style)

The iconic Hetfield-style riff is built entirely on the low E string with power chord punches. The rhythm is where all the work is: gallop (down-down-up), off-beat accents, sudden stops. Start at 60 BPM and do not move up until every note speaks cleanly with palm muting on.

E5 power chord -- the backbone of metal rhythm. Strings 6 and 5, fret 7. Mute strings 4-1 with the side of your first finger.

Gallop riff: E5 on beat 1 (open low E run), then A5 at fret 7. Play with heavy palm muting on all six notes of the run.

3. The Rhythm -- Gallop and Syncopation

The gallop is a triplet-feel rhythm: long-short-short (one-and-uh, two-and-uh). It sounds like a horse at full canter -- hence the name. Almost every Metallica riff from Ride the Lightning onward uses this feel. The picking pattern is typically down-down-up per gallop unit.

The syncopated chug (Pantera style, think "Cowboys from Hell") puts accents on off-beats and uses sudden silences (rests) that hit as hard as the notes. Learn to treat silence as a weapon.

1Palm-Mute Gallop Drill

Setup: High gain, bridge pickup, amp gain at 7+.

Step 1: Set metronome to 60 BPM. Each click = one quarter note.

Step 2: Play the low E string (open) as: long (quarter) - short-short (two eighths). That's one gallop. Repeat for 4 bars, all palm muted.

Step 3: On beat 3 of every bar, punch E5 (fret 7 power chord) -- lifting the palm mute just for that one hit, then back on.

Step 4: Speed up by 5 BPM increments. Target: clean gallop at 160 BPM.

4. Metal Lead -- The Phrygian Sound

Metal lead guitar lives in two scales: the minor pentatonic (which you know from Book One) and the Phrygian mode. Phrygian is what gives metal its dark, Spanish-sounding flavor -- it's a natural minor scale with a flatted 2nd degree. That b2 (one half step above the root) is the most characteristic note in metal soloing.

E Phrygian uses the notes: E - F - G - A - B - C - D. Notice F natural (not F#). That F note, bent up to F# and back, is the signature metal-lead move.

E Phrygian scale -- box position. The b2 (F natural, string 1 fret 1) is the note that gives metal its menace. Run it top to bottom, then practice the bend from F to F#.

Phrygian position 2 -- starting from the A string. Connects to position 1 above. Solo vocabulary lives in the upper half of this shape.

5. The Pinch Harmonic

Zakk Wylde, Dimebag Darrell, and Kirk Hammett all use pinch harmonics to add screaming overtones to sustained notes. Technique: hold the pick so only the very tip extends past your thumb. When you strike the string, let your thumb's flesh lightly touch the string a fraction of a second after the pick hits. The exact spot on the string where you pick determines which harmonic you get -- move your picking hand closer to or farther from the bridge while you hold a fretted note. When you hit the right spot + right touch, the note jumps an octave (or more) and screams.

This takes time. Practice on the B string, fret 7, with high gain. Look for the "sweet spots" -- they're every few centimeters along the string.

6. Three Essential Metal Riffs to Learn

Black Sabbath "Iron Man" style riff. Slow, heavy, simple power chords moving by half and whole steps. The grandfather of all metal riffs -- entirely about feel and tone.

Cowboys from Hell style intro riff. Syncopated chug on the low E followed by a power chord punch. The silence after the chug is written into the riff -- don't fill it.

Four Horsemen / Sanitarium style gallop riff. A5 - E5 - D5 movement with palm-muted gallop rhythm. Thumb palm position is critical here.

7. Metal Tone Setup

The riffs above all live or die by amp tone. For metal -- Amp: High gain channel or overdrive pedal into a clean amp. EQ: Scoop the mids slightly (not all the way -- total mid scoop sounds thin). Boost lows and upper-highs. Pickup: Bridge humbucker. Guitar: Low action, heavier strings (.010-.046 or .052 for tuned-down). Tuning: Standard E, drop-D, or Eb standard.

2Drop D Tuning Drill

Drop D tunes the low E string down one whole step to D. Single-finger barre across strings 6-5-4 = instant power chord.

-- Single-finger D5 barre (fret 0, strings 6-4)
-- Slide that barre to fret 2 for E5, fret 5 for G5, fret 7 for A5
-- Gallop riff: open D5 (muted) x4 -- slam G5 -- then A5

✓Chapter 1 Checklist







You have completed Book Three -- Genre Masterclass. More chapters coming.

← PreviousNext →
Book Three · Chapter 4

⚠ Death Metal Patterns

☢
GoalMaster the four core death metal techniques: tremolo picking, chromatic chugging, blast-beat synchronization, and the dark harmonic language that makes this genre brutally precise.

Death metal is not heavy metal played faster. It is a completely different discipline -- every technique is pushed to its mechanical limit, and sloppiness that "gets by" in classic rock is immediately exposed. The upside: learning death metal patterns will make your alternate picking, muting precision, and fretboard coordination dramatically better across all genres. This chapter is inspired by DUNN Metal Guitar Lessons, one of the clearest death metal tutorial channels available.

Ear anchors: Hammer Smashed Face (Cannibal Corpse), Pull the Plug (Death), Raining Blood (Slayer -- the death-adjacent thrash blueprint), Carved in Stone (Obituary). Listen specifically for how the picking hand never stops moving during tremolo sections.

1. Alternate Picking -- the Engine Behind Everything

Death metal begins with strict alternate picking: down-up-down-up, every single note, no exceptions. If you "cheat" with consecutive downstrokes you will never reach death metal tempos. The pick angle matters -- a slight slant (not perpendicular to the string) reduces friction and lets you move through strings faster.

Start on the low E string. Set your metronome to 80 BPM and play 16th notes (4 picks per beat). Your picking hand should feel like a spring -- relaxed, not forced. Tension is the enemy of speed. As you increase tempo the motion gets smaller, not larger. At 180+ BPM the pick barely moves.

Alternate picking drill on the low E string. Every dot is one pick stroke. Play all 8 notes as 16th notes: D-U-D-U-D-U-D-U. Start at 80 BPM and do not increase until it feels effortless.

2. Tremolo Picking -- Controlled Blur

Tremolo picking is alternate picking taken to its fastest sustainable limit on a single note or string, producing a sustained blur of pitch. In death metal it is applied to melodies, riff accents, and entire passages. The key: tremolo picking is NOT frantic. It is controlled. Your picking elbow stays still -- the motion comes from the wrist and forearm rotation only. Practice on one note for two minutes straight without stopping. The goal is evenness, not speed.

Tremolo picking exercise: hold each note and pick continuously for 4-8 beats. String 1 (purple, high E) is the hardest -- your pick must be equally clean on thin strings.

1Tremolo Endurance Drill

Step 1: Set metronome to 80 BPM. Fret string 6 at fret 5 (A). Tremolo pick for exactly 60 seconds without stopping. Count how many times your picking hand "resets" -- should be zero.

Step 2: Move to string 1 (high E), fret 12. Same 60-second drill. Thin strings need lighter pick pressure.

Step 3: Tremolo on string 6 fret 5 for 2 beats, slide to fret 7 while still picking. Do NOT stop picking during the slide. This is the core death metal melodic move.

Target: Clean, even tremolo at 200 BPM 16th notes.

3. Chromatic Chugging Patterns

Death metal riffs frequently use chromatic movement -- notes one half step apart. The classic pattern: palm-muted open low E followed by a chromatic climb: E - F - F# - G, or descents like G - F# - F - E. The muting must be consistent -- every chromatic note should sound exactly as tight as the open string chug.

Pattern 1: Chromatic ascent. Open E (PM) x2 -- fret 1 -- fret 2 -- fret 3. Drop back to open. All palm muted. This is the riff engine of dozens of death metal songs.

Pattern 2: Chromatic descent from G. Fret 3 -- fret 2 -- fret 1 -- open. Repeat. The descending version sounds darker and more menacing.

4. The Palm-Mute + Open String Alternation

The most recognizable death metal rhythm: tight palm-muted chugs alternating with open power chord hits. The open hit lifts the palm mute completely for one stroke, creating a bloom of ring, then the mute returns immediately. Practice the transition slowly: PM PM PM OPEN PM PM PM OPEN, counting the open hit as a rhythmic accent.

PM/open alternation riff. Open E chugs (PM) x3, then A5 power chord hit (no PM) on beat 4. The A5 accent carries the musical message -- make it ring fully.

2Mute Precision Drill

Set metronome to 100 BPM. Play: PM PM PM OPEN (x4 bars). Then shift accent: PM PM OPEN PM. Then: PM OPEN PM PM. Moving the accent while keeping all other notes tight is the death metal rhythm vocabulary in one exercise. Record yourself -- PM notes should be identical, OPEN note dramatically louder.

5. Drop D Power Chord Riffs

Death metal often uses drop D tuning (low E tuned down to D). A single-finger barre across strings 6-4 gives instant power chords, enabling fast movement impossible in standard tuning. Signature riff structure: establish a root chord, then move by minor 2nds (one fret), minor 3rds (three frets), or tritones (six frets -- "the devil's interval").

Drop D power chord movement: D5 (open barre) -- F5 (fret 3) -- Eb5 (fret 1) -- back. Each barre = one finger across strings 6, 5, 4.

Tritone riff: D5 (open) -- Ab5 (fret 6). The tritone is exactly half an octave -- the most dissonant interval in Western music. Death metal uses it constantly. Tight PM on D5, full ring on Ab5.

6. The Phrygian Dominant Scale

Death metal leads use Phrygian Dominant -- the fifth mode of the harmonic minor scale. E Phrygian Dominant: E - F - G# - A - B - C - D. The G# (raised 3rd) gives it a Middle Eastern flavor. The move from F to G# is the signature exotic death metal melodic line.

E Phrygian Dominant box position. The raised 3rd (G#) separates this from regular Phrygian. Use for melodic death metal leads.

7. DUNN Playlist Study Guide

The DUNN Metal Guitar Lessons playlist (75+ videos) teaches death metal patterns easy to advanced. Work through in this order:

  • Basic chromatic chug patterns -- single-string chromatic movement with PM
  • Open-string accent riffs -- PM chug + open hit at various rhythmic positions
  • Drop D power chord sequences -- single-finger barre movement, minor 2nd and 3rd jumps
  • Tremolo riff patterns -- tremolo picking on melodic fragments, not just single notes
  • Tritone riffs -- the devil's interval as full riff engine
  • Phrygian Dominant melodic lines -- melodic death metal lead vocabulary
3Full Death Metal Pattern Drill

Pattern A: Palm-muted open E x8 (16th notes at 120 BPM), then D5 power chord (drop D fret 0) for 2 beats. Repeat 4 bars.

Pattern B: Open E PM x2, fret 1 PM, fret 2 PM, fret 3 PM, then open E (lift mute). 2 bars.

Pattern C: Chug open E x6, then hit Ab5 at fret 6 for one downstroke. Mute immediately.

Chain A-A-B-B-C-C for one 8-bar death metal verse. Start at 80 BPM, increase 10 BPM each week. Target: 160 BPM.

✓Chapter 4 Checklist








Book Three -- Genre Masterclass continues. More chapters coming.

← PreviousNext →
Book Three · Chapter 5

🔴 Chugging -- Up & Down the Fretboard

⚡
GoalBuild a vocabulary of chugging patterns that move up and down the neck -- from single-string runs to traveling power chord sequences -- so your riffs go somewhere instead of sitting in one spot.

Most beginners learn to chug in one place -- open low E, maybe one or two power chord punches, repeat. That is a starting point, not a destination. The players who write memorable riffs move their chugs around the neck, use the contrast between different string registers, and treat the palm mute like a volume knob -- turning it on and off to shape the dynamics of a whole riff. This chapter is entirely about making your chugs travel.

1. The Three Palm Mute Settings

Before you move anywhere on the neck you need to own three distinct muting levels:

Heavy PM (full chug): Palm heel sitting firmly just in front of the bridge saddles. Tight, percussive thud with clear pitch. This is your baseline chug tone.

Light PM (semi-open): Palm barely grazing the strings -- most of the pitch, slight bloom. Used on sustained notes within a riff for variation without going fully open.

Open (no PM): Palm lifts completely. Full ring, full sustain. The accent hit -- the note that "speaks" in the riff.

Practice cycling through all three on the same note before moving anywhere. If you cannot hit heavy/light/open on demand, fretboard travel will sound sloppy because position shifts will accidentally change your muting level.

1Three-Level Mute Drill

Metronome at 80 BPM. On the open low E string, play 4 beats heavy PM, 4 beats light PM, 4 beats open. Repeat without stopping for 3 minutes. Picking hand stays locked near the bridge -- only the heel contact changes. Do this until switching levels feels automatic.

2. Single-String Chug Runs

The simplest fretboard travel: stay on one string and move up or down by interval. These run best on string 6 (low E, red) and string 5 (A, yellow). Key rule: the palm mute position never changes as you move up the neck -- your fretting hand travels, your picking hand stays anchored near the bridge.

Whole-step chug run on string 6: open E -- fret 2 (F#) -- fret 4 (G#) -- fret 5 (A). All heavy PM. Play each position for 2 beats then move. The pitch rises but the chug feel stays identical.

Half-step chromatic chug run: open E through fret 5 (A), one fret at a time, 2 beats each. Slow-building menace -- each half step raises the tension by one notch.

2Ascent-Descent Loop

Metronome 100 BPM. Play: open (2 beats) -- fret 2 (2 beats) -- fret 4 (2 beats) -- fret 5 (2 beats) -- fret 4 (2 beats) -- fret 2 (2 beats) -- back to open. All heavy PM. This 12-beat loop is a riff on its own -- it rises, peaks, and falls. That arc is what separates a riff from a drill.

3. Power Chord Chugs Traveling Up the Neck

Move a power chord up the neck while chugging and you get instant harmonic movement. Most useful travel intervals: minor 3rd jumps (3 frets), 4th jumps (5 frets), 5th jumps (7 frets). These all come from the pentatonic scale -- they all sound musical even in isolation.

Power chord ascent by minor 3rds: E5 (fret 7) -- G5 (fret 10) -- Bb5 (fret 13). Chug PM x3 on each, then punch the next chord open on beat 4 to signal the change. The punch previews the move.

Power chord descent: A5 (fret 12) -- G5 (fret 10) -- E5 (fret 7) -- D5 (fret 5). Chug PM x2 on each, then one open punch before moving. Descending feels heavier than ascending -- the riff "falls" toward the root.

4. String-Jumping Chug Patterns

String jumping: chug on one string, jump to a different string for an accent, return. The low string churns while a higher string delivers a melodic moment. The muting challenge -- when you jump to a higher string, the lower strings must be silenced by your fretting hand fingers.

Basic string jump: chug open E (string 6, PM) x3 -- accent string 4 fret 5 (A, no PM) -- back to chug. The A note is a 4th above the E root -- powerful interval used in countless metal intros.

Two-string jump riff: string 6 open (PM) x2 -- string 5 fret 2 (B, PM) x2 -- string 4 fret 2 (E, open punch). Three strings, three registers. Funnel-shaped texture from thick to thin.

3String Jump Accuracy Drill

Slow to 60 BPM. Play: string 6 PM x4 -- string 4 open x1 -- repeat. Each jump skips 2 strings. Count out loud on every beat. Increase 5 BPM per session. Your picking hand stays in one place -- only the pick angle changes to target the new string.

5. Cool Chug Sounds to Know

The chug-and-slide: Chug a fretted note PM, then slide up 2 frets while still picking. Pitch smears upward mid-chug. Used heavily in groove metal -- Pantera, Lamb of God.

The gallop-travel: Gallop rhythm (long-short-short) but move the chord on the long note each bar. Short notes always return to open low E. The riff "gallops through" different chord shapes.

The octave accent: Chug string 6 open, then jump to the same note one octave up on string 4 fret 2 (E) for the accent. Same pitch, completely different texture -- sounds massive on a distorted amp.

Drop D single-finger travel: In drop D, one finger barres strings 6-4 for any power chord. Slide that barre up or down -- zero picking-hand shift, completely smooth fretboard travel. Best way to build fast traveling riffs.

Octave accent: low E open (string 6, red, PM chug) then E octave (string 4 fret 2, blue, open punch). Lock your eyes on the blue string for the accent -- your picking hand targets where you look.

Traveling riff map: the three main positions -- open E (home chug), D5 at fret 5 (drop), E5/G5 at frets 7 and 10 (peak). All connected by palm-muted open E chugging between moves.

6. The 4-Bar Traveling Riff Template

Bar 1: Chug open E PM x6, punch E5 (fret 7) open on beats 3-4.
Bar 2: Slide to D5 (fret 5) chug PM x4, punch G5 (fret 10) open on beat 4.
Bar 3: Chug G5 PM x4, drop to open E x2, punch string 4 fret 5 (A) open.
Bar 4: Open E chug PM x8 -- full bar, heavy tension reset back to bar 1.

That loop has ascending movement (E to G), a string jump accent (string 4), and a full-bar reset that builds anticipation. This is the skeleton of a real metal intro.

4Build Your Own Traveling Riff

Write a 2-bar riff with these constraints: (1) must start and end on open E chug, (2) must visit at least one fretted position above fret 5, (3) must include one string jump to a non-E string, (4) must use at least two PM levels. Play it at 80 BPM until smooth. Record on your phone. Does it go somewhere? Does it have a shape? If yes -- you just wrote a riff.

✓Chapter 5 Checklist








Book Three -- Genre Masterclass continues. More chapters coming.

← Previous
